"I don't know. Let me figure out what's happening."

"You look stressed about this."

"Because I am. This isn't—Leo's doing something I didn't agree to."

"Well, let us know. Mic already started looking up what to wear to a gala."

Jay tried calling Leo three more times during his workout. Voicemail every time.

By afternoon, Jay had forced himself to set aside his anxiety about the invitations. He had a concert to attend. An evening with Ian. He couldn't let Leo's schemes ruin it.

He dressed in his gray tailored three-piece suit, the onyx cufflinks Ian had given him for his birthday. He checked himself in the mirror three times, adjusting the waistcoat, smoothing his hair. He could almost pass for someone who belonged in Ian’s world, but the stiffness in his posture exposed him—always out of place, always the impostor.

Ian arrived at four. He was wearing a dark navy suit, perfectly cut, and he looked effortlessly elegant, something Jay suspected he’d never achieve himself. They headed down to Ian's car, and Jay tried to keep his thoughts off Leo and the invitations.

"You look very sharp," Ian said.

"Thank you. So do you."

They drove to a restaurant in the city centre, a place with no sign outside and no prices printed on the menu. Jay had been to similar places with Ian before, but he still felt the discomfort of it—the knowledge that he was performing a version of himself that didn't quite fit.

The morning's revelation about the invitations hadn't helped. He kept wondering what else Leo had done without consulting him.

"You're quiet," Ian observed after they'd ordered.

"Just taking it in," Jay said, which wasn't entirely a lie.

"If you'd rather go somewhere else—"

"No, this is fine. It's nice."

"You look like you're preparing for battle." 

"Do I?"

"A little. Relax. It's just lunch."

"Fancy lunch."

"Still just lunch."

Jay tried to loosen the tension in his shoulders. Ian was trying to put him at ease, and the least Jay could do was meet him halfway.

After lunch, they drove to the concert hall. Ian found parking nearby, and they walked the last few blocks.

"We're early. I wanted to show you the area. There's a sculpture garden just past the hall that's worth seeing."

They walked through streets lined with elegant old buildings, art galleries, expensive boutiques. The sculpture garden was small but beautiful, filled with modern pieces in stone and metal. Jay followed Ian through it, trying to appreciate what he was seeing, but he felt conspicuously out of place.

The concert hall itself was magnificent. Soaring ceilings, marble floors, chandeliers that cast warm light over everything. People milled about in the lobby, all of them looking comfortable. Jay's suit was good, but he still felt provincial, like someone who'd wandered into the wrong room. Ian must have noticed. He touched Jay's elbow lightly, guiding him toward a quieter corner.

"The acoustics are supposed to be exceptional. The hall was renovated five years ago. They reinforced the structure while preserving the original aesthetic."

"It's beautiful."

"You seem uncomfortable."

"I'm just not used to places like this."

"It's just a building. Just people. You belong here as much as anyone."

Jay wanted to believe that. He tried to relax into Ian's reassurance, but Ian's eyes had that look like he was somewhere else entirely. They found their seats, and the concert began.

The music was lovely, intricate and well performed, and Jay tried to focus on it, but he kept noticing Ian's stillness, the way his attention seemed to drift even when his eyes were on the stage. Jay didn't know what was wrong, but he felt a change.

Dinner afterwards was at another elegant restaurant, this one smaller, more intimate. Ian had reserved a table, and they sat across from each other in the low light. Jay ordered something simple, still feeling full from lunch. Ian did the same, but he barely touched his food.

Jay tried to lift the mood, telling Ian a story about Rin accidentally emailing the entire company instead of just Luc, then another about Max's vacation photos featuring an alarming number of seagulls. He kept his tone light, trying to draw Ian out without being obvious about it. Ian smiled, but he'd grown even quieter, as if he was half in another place.

Jay wondered if he'd been too awkward at lunch, too visibly uncomfortable at the concert. Maybe he'd spoiled the evening without realizing it.

So he tried harder. Questions about the music, about the composers, about anything that might draw Ian back out of wherever he'd retreated to. Ian responded, but it sounded like he didn't want to burden Jay with whatever he was thinking.

They both forced tenderness through the tension, pretending nothing was wrong.

When Ian dropped Jay off, they said goodnight like always. Whatever happened, it felt too tangled to address directly.

Sunday was gray, both outside and in Jay's head.

Jay tried to distract himself with cleaning, with reading, with anything that would stop him from replaying the previous evening and trying to identify the problem. He couldn't pinpoint the moment things had turned sour, but they had. Ian had been fine at lunch, a little distant at the concert, and progressively quieter through dinner. Had he said something wrong? Reminded Ian too starkly of the distance between their worlds? He felt like he failed some test he hadn't known he was taking.

In his own apartment, Ian sat on the couch and brooded.

Sam Connelly had loved classical music. Ian had been so excited to share that part of his life, to bring Sam to concerts, to discuss compositions and interpretations. Sam had seemed equally enthusiastic, engaging fully.

All of it a lie. All of it part of the performance Sam had maintained to gain Ian's trust.

And yesterday, sitting in that concert hall with Jay, the memories had intruded. Sam's voice commenting on the orchestra. Sam's hand on Ian's arm during a particularly moving passage. The complete fabrication of shared interest and mutual understanding.

Ian had tried to push it away, to stay present with Jay, but the ghost had won.

Jay had noticed and tried so hard to fix it, and Ian had felt too guilty to let him, too aware that his own past was poisoning something that should be simple and good.

He hadn't meant to let it intrude. Jay deserved better than to be overshadowed by Ian's past, but Ian hadn't been able to shake it. And now Jay probably thought Ian had regretted the entire outing.

Monday morning began with a phone call from Ema that shattered whatever fragile calm Jay had managed to build.

"Jay! I got an invitation!"

"What?"

"To the party! Ian's birthday party! Leo sent me one! I've been messaging him on social media and I told him I wanted to come and he said yes!"

Jay's blood went cold. "Ema, you can't—"

"I know it's fancy, but I'll be so good, I promise. I'll dress up and I won't embarrass you. Please, Jay, please let me come. It's a real gala. I've never been to anything like that."

"Ema, no. You're sixteen. You can't go to something like this."

"But Leo said—"

"I don't care what Leo said. You're not going."

 "Jay, please. I really want to."

"I said no. I'll talk to Mom."

"Mom already said I can't, but if you say it's okay—"

"It's not okay. I'm sorry, but it's not happening."

There was a long silence, and then Ema started crying.

"I have to go. I'll call you later." Jay hung up and immediately dialed his mother.

"I know," she said before he could speak. "She showed me the invitation. I already told her no."

"Good. Thank you."

"She's very upset."

"I know. But she's a child. She doesn't belong at something like this."

"I agree. I'll talk to her again."

Jay hung up and tried Leo's number. Still voicemail.

Ten minutes later, Ema's first text arrived: "Please, Jay. I'll do anything. I won't ask for anything else ever again." By the time he arrived at the office, Jay was exhausted and stressed. The texts from Ema hadn't stopped.

Ian appeared at his desk mid-morning. "Do you have a moment?"

"Of course."

They went to Ian's office. Ian closed the door and turned to face him.

"Is something wrong?"

"No. Why?"

"You seem distracted."

"I'm fine. Just a family thing. Nothing serious."

Ian looked like he wanted to press, but he didn't. "If you need anything—"

"I know. Thank you."

Jay returned to his desk and tried to appear normal, but the texts kept coming and Leo remained unreachable. He hated worrying Ian, especially when Ian was already dealing with whatever had been bothering him on Saturday, but he couldn't explain the Ema situation without admitting Leo's party planning had gone completely off the rails. And he couldn't do that without potentially stressing Ian about his own birthday.

And Ian kept glancing at him from his office. He was certain now. Saturday had been his fault. He'd spoiled the evening and now Jay was hurt. 

By the end of the day, Ema had sent six more texts, each one sadder and more pleading than the last.

Tuesday's jog was tense. Jay couldn't shake the stress, and it showed in the way he ran—too fast at first, then flagging, his breathing uneven.

Halfway through, Ian said, "I wanted to apologize for Saturday."

"What?"

"I wasn't good company. I'm sorry if I made the evening uncomfortable."

"You didn't. I thought I did. I was worried I'd been too awkward."

"You weren't. I let something distract me and I made you uncomfortable. I'm sorry."

"It's okay. Really. I wasn't upset about that."

"Then what were you upset about?"

Ian looked distressed, and Jay felt guilty. He didn’t want to trouble him with Leo’s messes, yet keeping it secret was clearly causing more damage than telling him outright.

"Leo sent invitations to people I didn't expect. Including my sister. Who now desperately wants to attend a formal gala with rich people and alcohol, and my mother and I both said no, and she's been texting me nonstop about how unfair I'm being."

"Leo invited your sister?"

"And my gym trainer. And his friends. People who barely know you. I've been trying to call him all weekend but his phone's off."

"I'll talk to him."

"No. Don't. This party is supposed to be good for you, not a source of stress. I'll deal with Leo myself. As soon as I can actually reach him."

"Jay, this is my party. I should—"

"You should enjoy it. That's all. Let me handle the rest."

Ian looked unconvinced but didn't argue further.

* * *

#### The most proper man

Leo remained unreachable, and Ema's texts kept coming—sad, disappointed, pleading. She promised to behave, to dress perfectly, to not embarrass him. She just wanted to go to a real gala. Jay's stress mounted. Every text felt like a small knife, guilt and frustration twisting together.

Ian noticed. He didn't push, but his concern was visible, and that made Jay feel worse.

By Friday, Jay was worn down. Ian took him to dinner again, but Jay was quiet, picking at his food.

"You're still upset," Ian said halfway through the meal.

"Leo's still unreachable. Ema's still texting. My mother's still angry. I don't know how to fix this."

"Maybe it's not such a bad idea."

"What?"

"Your sister attending. If she's properly supervised."

"You don't understand. My mother will never allow it."

"Why not?"

"She just won't. She doesn't want Ema around wealthy people."

"It wouldn't be like that. You'd be there. I'd be there. We'd watch over her. It's a birthday party, nothing nefarious."

"You don't know my mother."

"No, but I know you. And I know you wouldn't let anything bad happen to your sister."

Jay wanted to believe it could be that simple. But he knew his mother's reasons for keeping distance from his world. The fear that her children might be exposed to people who might hurt them.

"I'm still not convinced," Jay said.

"Think about it. That's all I'm asking."

For the rest of the evening, Jay's mind was tangled with Ema and Leo and the party that was supposed to be simple but had become increasingly complicated. When Ian dropped him off at their building, Jay felt no closer to a solution.

"I'm losing my mind," Jay announced at the gym on Saturday.

"What happened now?" Rus asked.

"Leo invited Ema, my sixteen-year-old sister. And she's furious I won't let her come. She won't stop texting me. My mother is losing her mind. Leo disappeared off the face of the earth. And apparently you got invited too, which—why? You met Ian once."

"Twice, technically. Once when we helped you move, once at your birthday party. And we RSVP'd to the party. Hope that's alright."

"You did?"

"Yeah. Mic is thrilled about the whole formal dress-up thing. He's already bought a new suit. Van's not really into it but Mic convinced him to come anyway. If you'd rather we didn't—"

Jay groaned and put his head in his hands. "I don't mind you being there. It's fine. Great, even. But my sister—she thinks it's going to be glamorous and exciting. My mother thinks it's going to be dangerous and inappropriate."

"If you bring her, I'll watch her. Personally. Like a hawk. Make sure nobody bothers her, make sure she stays out of trouble. But honestly, if you have such a high opinion of Ian—and you clearly do—then the party shouldn't really pose any threats to young girls, right? Ian would never allow anything inappropriate to happen."

"That's... actually a good point."

"I know. I have them occasionally."

"Ian would be horrified if anyone behaved inappropriately around Ema. He'd shut it down immediately."

"Exactly. So maybe your mom's fears aren't as relevant here as she thinks."

Rus was right. Nothing untoward would happen under Ian's watch, but that didn't make convincing his mother any easier.

Back at home that afternoon, Jay called his mother. The conversation went on for over an hour.

"Mom, listen. I know you're worried—"

"Worried? Jay, she has no business at an event like that."

"I'll be there. Ian will be there. My friend Rus already promised to watch her specifically."

"You don't understand what these people are like."

"I work with these people every day."

"That's different. You're an adult. You can handle yourself. Ema is a child who thinks everything is glamorous and exciting. She doesn't see danger until it's too late."

"She's going to resent us both if we say no."

"Let her resent me. I'm her mother. I'm supposed to protect her, not give her what she wants."

"What if it's just a party and she has a nice time and comes home safe?"

"And what if it isn't? What if someone pays too much attention to her? What if she gets ideas about a life she can't have?"

Jay didn't know how to answer that. The fear in his mother's voice was too deep, too rooted in experiences he didn't fully understand.

"Can we just think about it? We don't have to decide right now."

"There's nothing to think about."

"Mom—"

"I have to go. We'll talk later."

She hung up before he could respond. Jay stared at his phone, exhausted by the argument he couldn't win and didn't entirely want to.

The final fitting was scheduled for that same afternoon. Jay arrived at the tailor's shop feeling wrung out from the phone call. The tailor greeted him with and led him to the fitting room. Three suits hung waiting—one black, severe and formal, the other two in lighter shades of charcoal and navy, classic and elegant.

"Try the black one first," the tailor instructed.

Jay changed and examined himself in the mirrors. The suit fit perfectly. Every line, every angle, every proportion exactly right.  The other two suits were equally perfect. Almost no adjustments needed. The tailor made notes, confirmed delivery dates, and sent Jay home with the knowledge that his collection of expensive, perfectly tailored suits was now comprehensive enough for any conceivable occasion.

The final week of August drifted by. Tia Larson's start date was confirmed for the first week of September, and Jay once again prepared onboarding materials.

The Tuesday jogs continued. Jay's form showed barely any improvement—he still struggled through the last third, still arrived at the bench breathing hard, but at least it was becoming less awkward now.

Friday dinner also happened naturally. Jay did his best not to mope about the party situation, though he couldn't help pouting dramatically.

"Leo is ruining my life."

"That seems excessive."

"He's been unreachable for two weeks. Two weeks. Who does that?"

"Leo, apparently. Though in fairness, Leo's always been erratic about communication."

"He invited a child to a formal gala without asking permission. He invited people I barely know. He disappeared for two weeks. I'm not being excessive."

"You really are upset with him."

"I'm furious with him."

"It's a little endearing."

"Don't encourage him by finding this amusing."

"For what it's worth, I think your sister would be safe at the party. But I understand your mother's concerns."

"Do you?"

"Protective instincts don't always align with logic. Sometimes fear is just fear, regardless of whether it's justified."

"She has reasons."

"I'm sure she does."

They didn't discuss it further. The evening continued pleasantly, and when Ian drove Jay home, the week didn't seem that gloomy anymore.

Sunday morning, Leo finally answered his phone.

"Jay! How are you?"

"How am I? How am I? Leo, I've been trying to reach you for two weeks!"

"I was traveling. My phone was off."

"You invited my sister to the party!"

"She seemed enthusiastic about it."

"She's sixteen!"

"So? She'll be supervised. What's the problem?"

Jay wanted to reach through the phone and strangle him. "The problem is you didn't ask me first. The problem is my mother is furious. The problem is you're making decisions about my family without consulting me."

"You're being melodramatic."

"I'm being—" Jay stopped, forced himself to breathe. "Leo, this isn't okay."

"Jay, nothing bad is going to happen. Ian is the most proper man in the district. Your sister will be at a party attended by respectable people, with you present, with Ian present, probably with half the office present. What exactly do you think is going to happen?"

Jay couldn't actually answer that. When Leo laid it out that way, his mother's fears did sound abstract.

"She might not even be allowed to come," Jay said finally.

"Then she won't. Problem solved. Look, I'm sorry I didn't ask first. But I thought it would be fine. And I was unavailable because I was abroad hunting for Ian's birthday gift. Which, by the way, is spectacular."

"What kind of gift?"

"Can't tell you. It's a surprise. But I had to go to three different countries to track it down."

"Three countries?"

"Worth every kilometre. Ian's going to love it."

After they hung up, Jay sat on his couch and stared at the wall. Three countries. Leo had traveled to three different countries to find Ian's birthday gift. Jay's gift suddenly felt desperately inadequate.

He stood up and went to his kitchen cupboards, pulling out jars and bottles and packets of herbs. The tinctures he'd made were looking good, but not special enough. Not when competing with whatever Leo had hunted down across three countries. 

He'd try harder. He opened his laptop and started ordering new ingredients. Alpine herbs, unusual spices, along with a dozen more jars. Things he could infuse quickly, experiment with, create variations. Different ratios, different bases, blind experimentation with the hope that something would turn out exceptional.

His cupboards were already overflowing, jars lined every available surface. Half of them eventually migrated to the basement storage when there was simply no room left in his apartment. But it still felt like it wasn't enough. 

He pulled up his laptop and resumed searching. Gifts for people who appreciate classical music. Concert tickets. Music boxes. First edition scores.

Everything was either too generic or required more time than Jay had left. He was starting to panic.

* * *

#### Tia Larson

September brought cooler mornings and the first hints of gold in the trees. Amy and Luc returned on Monday, both looking refreshed.

Kim and Ela departed together, no longer pretending their joint leave was coincidental. They'd chosen a mountain getaway in the late summer warmth, Kim showing photos of the charming cabin they'd rented while Ela made comments about finally getting proper rest.

The office reshuffled to accommodate the changes. And into this transition walked Rin's salvation.

"Thank god you're back. I've been surrounded by people who either ignore me or terrify me for two weeks," Rin said, appearing at Luc's desk within seconds.

Luc set down his bag with theatrical gravity. "Fear not, young apprentice. Your mentor has returned from his journey across distant lands. Did you miss me?"

"Yes." The answer was completely earnest.

"Well then, gather round young one, and let me tell you tales of my adventures. There were bears. Possibly. I saw something large and brown. Could have been a dog."

Jay watched from his desk as Rin pulled up a chair and Luc launched into an increasingly exaggerated account of his camping trip. Within minutes, Rin was laughing.

Jay walked over. "Welcome back."

"Jay! You survived without me. I'm almost offended by how well the office functioned in my absence."

"We barely managed. There was crying. Possibly mine."

"As there should be. How was August? Anything exciting happen?"

"Define exciting."

"Anything that would make a good story."

"Then no. Completely boring. We just worked."

"Lies. You're a terrible liar." Luc turned to Rin. "Was he lying?"

"Absolutely," Rin said immediately.

"Traitor," Jay said dramatically.

They bantered for a few more minutes before Jay returned to his desk. The office felt more balanced with Luc back—his voice filling spaces that had seemed too quiet.

But Monday also brought another addition.

Tia Larson arrived at nine o'clock. Jay and Ian met her in the lobby. She was tall, straight-backed, with short dark hair and a handshake that could crush stone. Her voice was crisp, her stride fast, and everything about her screamed military background.

They'd coordinated the introduction the same way they had for every recent hire—brief but welcoming.

"Tia Larson. Pleased to be here."

"Jay Hall. This is Ian Hagen, CFO."

"Sir. Thank you for the opportunity."

"Welcome to Turner & Associates. Jay will handle your initial orientation. HR will take over for the standard lectures."

They gave her a short tour—the floor layout, the break room, the various departments. Jay tried to balance friendliness with caution. She seemed direct, nothing like Gal. But Jay had learned that first impressions could be deceiving. He'd keep his guard up.

After the brief introduction, he handed her off to HR. Two days of lectures about company policy, benefits, procedures, the standard onboarding process that every new hire endured.

The air was cooler during the Tuesday's jog. Jay noticed it right away—autumn was coming, summer finally loosening its grip.

"How's Tia doing?" Ian asked.

"Hard to say. She's only been here two days, and most of that was HR. I'll know more after this week. I think she will be fine, though. She seems focused. Not the type to cause problems."

They ran in silence for a while, and then Jay said, "It's strange how quickly the seasons change. Feels like summer just started."

"It does."

"Another few weeks and it'll be too cold for comfortable evening runs."

"We'll adjust the schedule. Earlier in the day, perhaps."

"Or move indoors."

"Indoor tracks are depressing."

"Says the man who spends seven days a week in a gym."

"That's different. That's disciplined training. Indoor track running is soul-crushing monotony."

"Fair."

"You must be getting more comfortable if you have this much energy to talk." 

Jay realized he was right. The run still required effort, but he wasn't gasping for air between words anymore. "I guess I'm getting better at this."

They finished the route without further conversation, but Jay felt pleased. He was still tired, still feeling the burn in his muscles, but it was already easier.

Tia adapted quickly through her first week. She was direct, asked questions when she needed clarification, and didn't seem prone to conflict. She completed the HR orientations and transitioned into actual work without drama.

Jay assigned her standard novice tasks—data verification, report formatting, simple analysis reviews. She handled everything well, working diligently through assignments. Her previous finance experience was evident in how quickly she grasped the nuances.

Rin appointed himself unofficial tour guide, showing her the office amenities.

"The coffee machine has twelve settings. It makes everything. Espresso, cappuccino, lattes. There's a setting for foam density. It's incredible."

"Noted," Tia said, amused.

"And the snacks. The snacks are important. The dark chocolate almonds are the best, but the dried mango is also really good. The sea salt crackers are phenomenal. The fruit smoothies are so-so, but Jay seems to love them."

"Essential information."

"I know. I take my responsibilities as tour guide very seriously."

Tia joined the lunch group Wednesday afternoon. Max had led an outing to a sandwich shop he claimed made the best pastrami in the city. The group that assembled included Jay, Tia, Max, Luc, Amy, Rin, Liv and Zoe.

They walked through cooling September air to a hole-in-the-wall shop with questionable decor and amazing smells. Tia integrated into the group without apparent intimidation, taking her place at the crowded table and participating in conversations.

"How are you finding the office so far?" Amy asked.

"Elegenat. Better coffee than my last position. The team seems solid. Everyone I've met has been helpful. Though I notice there's one person who hasn't shown his face—Ray, was it?"

"Ray is... particular," Luc said diplomatically.

"He's antisocial," Max corrected. "Brilliant analyst, terrible human interaction skills. Don't take it personally."

"I don't take much personally."

They ate and talked through lunch. Tia answered questions about her background—military logistics, private sector finance. She asked questions in return about office culture, project dynamics.

Jay watched Tia throughout the meal. She was confident but not domineering, direct but not aggressive. Nothing like Gal. But Jay's wariness remained. First impressions could deceive. He'd stay watchful.

Friday afternoon, Max organized welcome drinks for Tia at their favourite bar. The invitation was casual—come if you want, no pressure, just a few hours of informal socializing.

Jay was comically stiff about the whole thing. He kept remembering Gal at similar gatherings, how normal everything had seemed right up until it wasn't. Ian attended reluctantly. He almost never came to these events, but felt obligated given Tia's position. 

They arrived at six to find Max, Luc, Amy, Zoe, Rin, and Tia already assembled around a large table. Liv appeared shortly after, elegant and smiling. Ray, predictably, never showed.

Ian ordered a single glass of wine. Jay got a beer. They sat at opposite ends of the table, surrounded by the cheerful noise.

Tia, it turned out, could drink like a sailor.

"Another round," she announced after finishing her second beer in twenty minutes.

"Already?" Luc looked impressed.

She proceeded to match Max drink for drink, then surpass him. Luc tried to keep pace and failed spectacularly. Within an hour, both men were visibly drunk while Tia looked unbothered.

"How are you still coherent?" Max asked.

"Superior constitution. Also, I'm pacing myself. You two are not."

Jay stayed on his first beer. Ian left after forty-five minutes. Jay noticed Ian checking the time repeatedly. He assumed Ian was simply unhappy about having to attend, counting down until he could make an exit.

What Jay didn't realize was that Ian was annoyed this gathering was cutting into their Friday dinner. Ian had come to depend on those evenings. This disruption felt disproportionately frustrating, though he tried to act unperturbed.

Liv left shortly after Ian, making graceful excuses and promising to see everyone Monday. The group grew smaller, louder, more relaxed.

By eight o'clock, Luc was actively drunk and Rin had appointed himself caretaker. Max was wobbling and Amy had taken charge of his coordination. Tia remained standing, amused by the situation.

"We should get them home," Jay said to Rin.

"Yeah. Probably."

Amy appeared at Jay's elbow, looking resigned. "I'll help with Max. You two take Luc?"

"Sure."

"I'll help with Max," Tia offered.

"All right then."

They split into groups. Jay and Rin each took one of Luc's arms, leading him to the street where they managed to get a taxi. Luc rambled the entire way about mountains and the meaning of life.

"You're a good friend, Jay. Really good. Don't let anyone tell you different."

"Thanks, Luc."

"And you, Rin. You're gonna be great. Just gotta believe in yourself."

"I'll remember that," Rin said seriously.

They delivered Luc to his apartment, made sure he got inside safely, and headed back out.

"That was eventful," Rin said.

"That's one word for it."

"Tia seems nice though. Different from—" Rin stopped.

"Different from Gal. You can say it."

"Yeah. Very different."

Jay got home at nine-thirty, exhausted and faintly amused. This was certainly an unusual social integration. Tia had held her own and made an impression.

Inside his apartment, Jay checked his bottles. The new experimental batches were progressing—alpine herbs turning the vodka a pale green, creating complex aromas. His gift was expanding beyond what he'd originally planned, but Leo's words still haunted him. Something expensive, something that required international travel.

Jay sealed the cabinet and sat on his couch.

He thought about Friday dinners. He wondered if they would continue, or if tonight had broken the streak. He thought about how much more time they spent together now compared to even a few months ago.

August had been intense, but it had also been full of Ian.

Jay wasn't sure if this was temporary—a strange month of circumstances that pushed them together—or if this was his new reality. If these patterns would continue or gradually fade back to how it was before.

He was too afraid to think about it more deeply. Too afraid that whatever this thing between him and Ian was, it was too fragile to survive scrutiny. That examining it too closely would make it collapse.

Two more weeks until Ian's birthday party.

Each passing day made him feel more scared than excited. The gift he'd been making felt inadequate. The party felt too large, too formal. His sister might or might not be allowed to attend, creating potential drama. Leo had invited half the city, apparently. And through it all, Jay would have to smile and pretend he was just Ian's assistant, just a colleague, just someone who happened to care about whether the evening went well.

* * *

#### Daring outfit

The weekend brought the party back into sharp focus. Two weeks away. Fourteen days.

Jay stood in his kitchen surrounded by jars of tinctures, tasting each one. This one was too bitter. That one lacked depth. Another was interesting but not special. Nothing felt good enough.

He revisited his notes, reading through combinations and steeping times, trying to identify where he'd gone wrong. The alpine herbs should have added complexity. But the results were just—fine. Not amazing.

Jay tried to be objective. These were nice liquors, but they were still homemade. Still amateur compared to what someone could buy from a proper distillery. Childish. That's what they felt like. A child's art project presented to an adult who deserved so much better.

He'd placed an order for crystal bottles—hand-carved, art deco style, breathtakingly expensive—hoping that presentation might compensate for the contents. He'd also ordered a wooden display box, something elegant to hold the collection. At least it would look presentable.

He opened his laptop and searched desperately for alternatives. The results were the same as always—expensive watches, first edition books, art pieces. Things Ian either already owned or wouldn't want. Things Jay couldn't afford even with his increased salary.

He closed the laptop and stared at the jars again. Two more weeks. Hopefully at least some of these mixtures would turn out good enough.

Sunday afternoon, Ema called again. Jay almost didn't answer—he'd received three texts from her that day already, each more dramatic than the last.

"Please, Jay. Please let me go to the party."

"Ema—"

"It might be my one chance. My one chance in my entire life to go to a real ball, like in books. To have a memory I'll keep forever. Please. I'll do anything. I'll follow every rule. I won't embarrass you. Please."

The desperation in her voice cracked something in him. She wanted magic and glamour and a single evening of feeling special.

"Alright."

"What?"

"You can come. But there are conditions."

"Anything!"

"You stay with me at all times. You don't drink alcohol. You don't go anywhere alone. You're respectful and polite. And if I say it's time to leave, we leave. No arguments."

"Yes! Yes, I promise. Thank you, Jay. Thank you so much!"

"And if anyone makes you uncomfortable, you tell me immediately."

"I will. I swear."

"I'll talk to Mom."

Ema burst into tears, but this time they sounded like relief. "Thank you. Thank you so much."

"Don't thank me yet. Mom still has to agree."

She hung up, and Jay sat staring at his phone, wondering what he'd just agreed to. Convincing his mother was harder. The conversation lasted nearly two hours, tense and circular.

"You don't understand what you're asking," she said.

"I do understand."

"Jay—" 

"I know how dangerous people can be. I know exactly how dangerous. If I had any doubts about the integrity of these people, any doubts at all, I would never consider bringing Ema. But I don't have doubts. These are good people. Nothing inappropriate will happen."

"You can't control everything."

"No, but I can control who she's near and where she goes. She'll be with me the entire time. Or with Rus, who's built like a mountain and extremely protective. She'll be safe, Mom. I promise."

"You really believe that?"

"I do."

"Fine. But if anything goes wrong—"

"It won't."

"If it does, this is on you."

"I know."

After they hung up, Jay received a text from Ema: "MOM SAID YES!!! CAN I BRING LAM???? THE INVITATION SAYS PLUS ONE!!!"

Jay stared at the message. Two sixteen-year-olds. This was going to be a disaster.

He texted back: "Yes. Same rules apply to her."

"THANK YOU THANK YOU THANK YOU I LOVE YOU!!!"

That evening, Jay tried on his suits.

The black one looked severe. The blue was better but still conservative. The gray made him think of that disastrous evening when someone had compared him to a funeral director. None of them felt right. They were all too serious, too staid.

He needed something different. Something stylish and modern and bold. Something that would let him stand among Ian's refined friends without feeling like a provincial pretender. 

Monday afternoon, Jay left work at five—earlier than he'd left on a Monday in months, and walked straight to Hathaway's Menswear.

The clerk greeted him immediately, smiling like they were old friends. "Good afternoon. Lovely to see you again."

"I need something for an event. Something dashing, but less formal."

"I assume you mean Mr. Hagen's birthday celebration?"

"How did you know that?"

"It's the talk of the district. Our shop is directly across from the Northlight Financial Center. I've seen you leave with Mr. Hagen on multiple occasions. And he's not exactly anonymous."

"Right. Of course."

"I'm Eli, by the way. I don't think I properly introduced myself last time."

"Jay Hall."

"A pleasure. So. Mr. Hagen's fortieth birthday gala. What kind of impression are you hoping to make?"

"I want to look stunning. Budget is not a concern."

"Excellent. Let's see what we can do."

For the next two hours, Jay tried on outfit after outfit. Eli pulled pieces Jay would never have chosen himself—jackets with interesting cuts, shirts in unexpected colors, pants that fit differently than anything Jay owned.

Finally, they settled on something daring. A blazer with a subtle floral pattern, elegant but eye-catching. A shirt with the top buttons deliberately left open, no tie. Pants that were, as Eli delicately put it, "very flattering to your proportions."

Jay stared at himself in the mirror. He looked stylish.

"You look spectacular. Trust me on this."

"The blazer isn't too much?"

"It's exactly enough. You'll stand out, but in the right way. You'll be the best-dressed person in that room."

"I doubt that." 

"Then you'll be in the top five. Which is impressive at an event where everyone will be dressed to kill. I know a hairstylist. Very talented. I'll arrange an appointment for you on the day of the party. He'll finish the look perfectly."

"That's—that's very generous."

"You're a good customer, Mr. Hall. And honestly, I'd love to see how this turns out."

The price was exorbitant but bearable. The occasion justified the cost.

He paid, arranged for tailoring adjustments, and exchanged phone numbers with Eli for coordination about the hairstylist.

"How can I repay you for all this help?" Jay asked.

Eli laughed. "An invitation to the party would be ideal. I've heard it's going to be the event of the season, but I'll settle for a bottle of good alcohol if you insist."

Jay hesitated. "I could probably arrange an invitation. If you're serious."

"Are you sure? I'd hate to impose."

"You've been incredibly helpful. And apparently half the city is already invited."

"Then I'd be honored. Thank you."

They shook hands and Jay left, feeling simultaneously relieved and anxious. One problem solved, at least partially.

But the gift still gave him a headache. And now he was wondering if inviting Eli was reckless. He barely knew him. What if he caused some kind of commotion? But he'd been helpful, and Jay appreciated it. And Eli had been nothing but professional and kind during all their interactions.

Tuesday evening, Ian met him at the park for their run. Jay arrived on time, still feeling slightly off-balance from the shopping trip.

They started jogging, and after a few minutes, Ian spoke.

"You left early yesterday."

"Yeah. I had an errand."

"Everything all right?"

"Fine. I just went shopping."

Jay decided to change the subject before Ian could probe further. "I convinced my mother to let Ema attend the party."

"You did?"

"After multiple conversations and a long list of conditions, yes. She'll be bringing her best friend Lam. Both are absolutely thrilled about attending a real ball. I'm completely responsible for their safety and behavior. No pressure."

"That was generous of you. I know you had reservations."

"I still have reservations. But Ema was so excited. And you were right—with proper supervision, it should be fine."

They ran in silence for a while. Then Jay spoke again, keeping his voice casual.

"Actually, speaking of the party—would you mind if I invited one more person? An acquaintance."

"Of course not. Invite whoever you'd like."

"Are you sure? I don't want to—"

"It's fine. The more people celebrating, the better."

"Thank you. I appreciate it."

"Of course."

That evening, Ian sat at his kitchen table with untouched dinner in front of him. Jay had invited someone to the party. An acquaintance. Another relationship that took precedence over whatever undefined thing existed between them.

It was unreasonable to feel jealous. Ian pushed his plate away and tried to talk himself out of the feeling, but the jealousy remained, petty and insistent, making him feel small and selfish and utterly pathetic.

He went to bed early, trying not to acknowledge how desperately he wanted to be the only person Jay thought to invite to important occasions. Trying not to admit how much he wanted Jay's time, attention, and affection to be his alone.

### Chapter 43: The celebration

#### Gold lions and carved dragons

Jay had never been to Leo's office before. He half-expected gold lions on the furniture, carved dragons, maybe even a water feature. Instead, he found a plain desk and a neat bookshelf. Almost aggressively normal.

"Something wrong?" Leo asked from behind his desk.

"No, I just—I expected something different."

"Like what? A throne?"

"Maybe."

Leo laughed. "My father has the throne room. I just needed somewhere to hide when he's being insufferable. Sit. What's this about?"

"I need an extra invitation. For someone named Eli."

Leo's eyebrow arched immediately, theatrically suspicious. "Eli? And who is this mysterious Eli?"

"Someone who's been kind to me. I want to pay them back."

"By inviting him to Ian's birthday party. Interesting choice."

"Is it a problem?"

"Not at all. I'll have an invitation prepared. Anything else?" 

"No. Thank you."

"You're very welcome. This is going to be a spectacular party, Jay. You'll see."

Jay left before Leo could ask any more questions.

That evening Jay called his mother. The conversation started with pleasantries—how was work, how was the apartment, had he been eating properly—before moving to the real reason for the call.

"Tim's birthday is coming up. I've been looking at cars, but the selection here is limited."

"I can look in the city. There's probably more variety."

"Would you? That would help so much."

They spent the next hour building a list: brands known for reliability, models that fit the budget, features that mattered. His mother wanted something safe. Jay wanted something that wouldn't break down every other month. Tim, apparently, wanted something that didn't look like it belonged to someone's grandfather.

"That narrows it down," Jay said dryly.

His mother laughed. "He's eighteen. He'll be happy with anything that runs and doesn't embarrass him."

"I'll see what I can find," Jay promised.

"Thank you, sweetheart. I don't know what we'd do without you."

Jay said goodbye and set his phone down, staring at the list of car models she had sent. He could do this. It was just research.

Friday dinners with Ian had become the highlight of Jay’s week. The previous one had been canceled for Tia’s welcome party, leaving him unsure whether the tradition would continue or if it had dissolved for good. When Ian stopped by his desk that afternoon to invite him out, relief rushed through him so fast he had to pause before answering.

The restaurant was different this time, but it had the same inviting atmosphere, the same sense of being in a place that felt like theirs.

“How’s your week been?” Ian asked once they’d ordered.

"Busy. I'm helping my mother look for a car. For Tim's birthday."

"That's thoughtful of you."

"The selection in their town is terrible. I figured I'd look here instead."

"I know someone."

"Of course you do."

"A reputable dealer," Ian continued, ignoring the faint sarcasm. "I can arrange an appointment. He owes me a favor."

"You don't have to—"

"It's no trouble. Give me the list of models you're considering, and I'll have him pull together options."

Jay pulled out his phone and showed Ian the notes from the call. 

"I'll call him. You should hear from him by Monday."

"Thank you."

"It's nothing," Ian said.

They ate and talked about work, neither of them paying real attention to the details. The joy of being together filled the space more than the words did. When they finally got up to leave, Jay looked at Ian with that helplessly tender expression he never noticed himself making, and Ian wanted to hold onto that look forever.

Saturday morning at the gym, Rus greeted Jay with amusement clear on his face.

"You won't believe this. Luc contacted me yesterday. About the party," Rus said.

"What about it?" Jay asked.

"Apparently he's arranging clothes for all of us. The whole gym group. He wants to make sure we're appropriately dressed. Do these rich people think we're going to show up with clubs and pelts?"

Jay laughed. "I don't think that's it."

"Well, we're getting fitted next week. It'll be a circus."

"Probably," Jay agreed.

Rus clapped him on the shoulder. "Don't worry about Ema, by the way. We've all got her back. She'll be safe."

"I know," Jay said, but it didn't stop the knot in his stomach from tightening every time he thought about it.

"Also, we got a group gift for your boss. It's good. Really good."

"What is it?"

"Not telling."

Jay groaned. "You're all impossible."

"We're delightful. Now stop stalling and get on the treadmill."

Saturday evening Jay was elbow-deep in tinctures when Leo appeared at his door without warning.

Jay sighed. "What do you want?"

"I brought Eli's invitation," Leo said, holding up an envelope. Then he stopped, taking in the kitchen. "What is all this?"

The kitchen looked like an apothecary had exploded. Bottles covered every surface—some full of dark liquid, others pale amber, still more in various stages of infusion. Jars of herbs and spices lined the counter. The beautiful crystal bottles Jay had ordered sat in their protective packaging, waiting to be filled. Notes covered in Jay's handwriting were scattered across the table.

"It's nothing."

"This is not nothing. This is a production facility." Leo picked up one of the experimental jars. "What are you making?"

Jay snatched it back. "Ian's birthday gift. And you're not touching anything."

"Can I try it?"

"No."

"Come on. Just a taste."

"No. It's for Ian. I won't let you taint Ian's gift with your meddling paws."

"You know, I'm starting to think you like him."

Jay stilled for a second before resuming his work. "He's my boss."

"Sure. Your boss. Who you make artisan liqueurs for."

"It's a gift. For his birthday."

"And it's very sweet. Painfully, devastatingly sweet."

Leo wandered around the kitchen, inspecting labels, asking questions about ingredients, and generally making himself at home. Eventually he dropped onto the couch and started rattling off details about the party. He left an hour later and Jay returned to his bottles.

Monday morning Jay slipped out of the office during a quiet stretch and crossed the street to the boutique. Eli was arranging a display near the window and looked up when the door chimed.

"Jay. Back so soon?"

"I brought the invitation for the party, if you still want to come."

Eli took the envelope as though it might dissolve in his hands. He opened it, read it, and then looked up with awe.

"You really invited me," Eli said.

"I said I would."

"I know, but—This is incredible. Thank you."

"It's just a party."

"It's not just a party. Not from what I've heard. What are you getting him?"

"Why do you want to know?"

"Because I need to get him something too. Standard courtesy for attending someone's birthday celebration. And while it's well-known that he likes good alcohol and classical music, you probably know him better. Any insights?"

"He's very into fitness and spends a lot of time at the gym. And he loves his work."

"That's not exactly helpful for gift ideas."

"I know. He's difficult to buy for. Everything he wants, he already has. Everything he needs, he's already arranged for himself."

"So you're going with alcohol?"

"Something like that. But classical music is always safe. Recordings, concert tickets, music-related items. He appreciates quality in that area."

They chatted pleasantly for a few more minutes—Eli asking about Jay's work, Jay learning that Eli had been at Hathaway's for four years and enjoyed the customer interaction. Jay was relieved that Eli seemed nice. Not pushy, not inappropriately curious, just friendly. Maybe inviting him hadn't been reckless after all.

The car dealership meeting happened later that week. Ian's contact was a middle-aged man with no interest in overselling. He listened to Jay's requirements, consulted his inventory, and promised to find something from the shortlist.

"Good price, excellent condition. I'll contact you with options."

"Thank you."

"Ian vouched for you. That's enough for me."

Another problem solved, another piece falling into place. And then, before Jay even registered the passing days, the final week before the party was already at his door.

Jay sat on his couch surrounded by three bottles. He had chosen the best of his tinctures, the ones with the clearest flavours. He had tucked them into the wooden box, and written a note describing each one. He stared at the box, willing his heart to slow down. He wanted this to be perfect. He wanted Ian to like it, to understand how much thought had gone into every step.

He definitely wanted too much.

* * *

#### Incandescent

It was raining on Tuesday. Not the gentle autumn drizzle that made everything seem romantic, but a relentless downpour that turned the streets into rivers.

Ian came to fetch Jay at six. "Ready to go?"

"Yeah."

As they drove through the rain, Jay watched the water run down the window and felt a weight in his chest.

"We have to cancel the run," Ian said.

"I was looking forward to it," Jay replied. He was unreasonably disappointed. Now the evening ahead was empty.

"So was I."

"Next week."

"Yes. Next week."

Ian pulled up outside Jay's building, and Jay hesitated before getting out.

"Thanks for the ride."

"Of course."

Jay walked inside, shook off his coat, and stood in the middle of his apartment feeling restless and gloomy. The rain drummed against the windows. It felt like a bad omen.

Wednesday afternoon, Leo cornered him at work.

"Jay. A word."

Jay followed Leo to his office, already suspicious.

"I've arranged everything for your sister's visit, just like I promised," Leo said, looking far too pleased with himself.

"What do you mean, everything?"

"The car will pick up Ema and her friend on Friday evening. They'll stay with you Friday night. Saturday morning, someone will collect them for the shopping trip—dresses, shoes, accessories. Then makeup and hair. They'll be delivered directly to the venue Saturday evening."

Jay stared at him. "Leo, that's too much—"

"Nonsense. They're your guests. They should arrive properly prepared."

"I can't afford—"

"It's my gift to them. And to you. Don't argue."

"The dresses need to be conservative. Age-appropriate."

Leo's smile was cherubic and utterly untrustworthy. "Of course."

"Leo."

"I promise. Nothing scandalous. They'll look elegant and proper."

Jay didn't believe him for a second, but arguing was pointless. Leo had already set everything in motion, and reversing it would cause more chaos than letting it proceed.

Wednesday evening, Jay called his mother.

"Everything's arranged. Leo's sending a car to pick them up Friday evening. They'll stay with me, then get taken care of on Saturday. Dresses, hair, makeup. Everything."

"That's very generous."

"I know it's a lot—"

"It's fine, Jay. If that's what's happening, then that's what's happening. I'll make sure they're ready Friday."

She sounded tired. Resigned. Jay wondered how many arguments she'd already had with Ema about this.

"Thanks, Mum."

After they hung up, Jay sat with his phone and stared at the wall. This was happening. Ema was coming. In three days, his sister would be at Ian's birthday party, dressed like she belonged in high society. He really hoped this wasn't a mistake.

Friday evening, the girls arrived in a flurry of giggles and enthusiasm. Jay opened the door to find Ema and Lam on the threshold, both talking at once, dragging enormous suitcases behind them.

"Jay!" Ema threw herself at him in a hug. "This is so exciting! The car was so fancy! There was bottled water and everything!"

"Hi, Ema."

"And Lam's never been to the city before, have you, Lam?"

Lam shook her head, wide-eyed. "It's huge."

"Come in," Jay said, stepping aside. "The bedroom is yours for tonight. I'll sleep on the couch."

"Really? You're the best brother ever!" Ema dragged Lam toward the bedroom. "Come on, let's unpack!"

The bedroom door flew open, the suitcases exploded across the bed, and clothes scattered everywhere. Music started playing from someone's phone. Shrieking laughter echoed through the flat.

Jay retreated to the balcony with a cup of tea. The rain had stopped, but the air still felt damp. Inside, the girls chattered endlessly about dresses and makeup and what the party would be like. Jay could hear them through the glass door.

The weekly Friday dinner with Ian was naturally cancelled. Too much happening, too many complications. Jay tried not to feel disappointed about it, but that absence felt like another bad omen.

He slept on the couch—or tried to sleep while giggling continued from his bedroom until midnight. Eventually exhaustion won and he drifted off to the sound of whispered conversations and occasional bursts of laughter.

Saturday morning, the car arrived at eleven sharp. Leo's driver was patient, waiting while the girls gathered their things and took final photos in Jay's apartment. Ema hugged Jay before leaving.

"I'll be good. I won't embarrass you."

"I know."

"And whatever you're wearing tonight, you're going to look amazing."

"Have fun."

He watched them drive away, feeling like a worried parent sending children off to their first day of school.

His phone buzzed. A message from Leo. "Small list of errands. Need these done before tonight."

The attached document was three pages long.

Jay scrolled through it in disbelief. Half the tasks were meaningless. Picking up items that could have been delivered. Confirming details that had already been confirmed twice. Dropping off materials at locations across the city. It was busywork. Pure sabotage.

He texted back: "Why?"

Leo's response came immediately: "Just trust me."

Jay didn't trust him at all. 

At three, Jay met Eli at the salon.

"Right on time. Come in. We've got two hours."

Two hours felt excessive. He sat in the chair and let Eli's team take over.

They started with his hair—washing, cutting, styling until it looked deliberately tousled but somehow elegant. Then the outfit. Jay changed into the floral blazer, the open-collar shirt, the perfectly fitted trousers. Eli adjusted the fit, made minute changes until everything sat exactly right.

"Perfect," Eli said finally, stepping back. "You look incredible."

"You're sure about the open collar?"

"I'm certain. It's sophisticated. Trust the vision. Ian Hagen is going to forget how to speak."

"That's not the goal."

"Then what is the goal?"

"To look... appropriate. Not like I'm trying too hard."

"You don't look like you're trying too hard. You look like you succeeded without trying at all." Eli handed him a small tube. "Lip balm. Use it before you leave tonight. Trust me."

"Thank you. For all of this."

"My pleasure. This is what I do. See you at the party?"

"See you there."

Jay left the salon and immediately started on the final errands. He took a taxi across town, picked up whatever Leo had ordered, then headed to another location for another pointless task. By seven, he was still running around, increasingly irritated.

He texted Rus: "Going to be late. Can you watch the girls?"

Rus replied immediately with a thumbs up.

Jay finished the last errand at seven-forty-five and finally headed to the venue. The party had started without him. He was over an hour late. He arrived at eight-ten, breathless and annoyed, holding the silver-and-gold wrapped package with its red ribbon.

The venue was stunning. A historic building with soaring ceilings, marble floors, and chandeliers that cast mystical light across everything. And people—so many people, dressed in expensive suits and gowns, holding glasses of champagne, talking and laughing.

The entrance hall opened onto a wide staircase that descended into the main space. Jay walked down, holding his gift, still catching his breath.

The crowd noticed him immediately. Conversations paused. Heads turned.

Jay walked down the staircase, acutely aware of every eye on him. He reached the bottom and headed straight for the gift table, setting his package down among the others. Then he scanned the room, looking for Ian.

Near the centre of the room, Ian turned. He froze. He forgot the party. Forgot the noise, the crowd, the obligation of the evening. He forgot why he'd been irritated moments before. Jay looked incandescent.

Jay crossed the room, weaving through the crowd, and stopped in front of Ian.

"Happy birthday," Jay said.

Ian stared at him, seemingly unable to speak.

"I know I'm late. Leo's errands. I'm sorry."

"You're—You look—"

"Ridiculous?"

"No. Not ridiculous."

The guests around them had diplomatically moved away, giving them space. Jay barely noticed.

"I hope you're enjoying the party. I know you didn't want this, but everyone here cares about you. They want to celebrate you. I wanted you to have a night where people remind you how much you matter. Because you do. You matter so much."

He hadn't meant to say it like that. Hadn't meant to be so earnest, so open. But the words were already out, and Ian was looking at him like Jay had just said something devastating.

"Thank you," Ian said.

Jay wanted to say more—to explain how important Ian had become, how much these two years had meant—but the words wouldn't come.

"I should let you get back to your guests," Jay said finally.

"Jay—"

"Happy birthday, Ian."

Jay stepped back, giving Ian space to return to the crowd. But Ian's eyes followed him, and Jay felt the weight of that attention even as he moved away.

* * *

#### Ian's world

Jay's first priority after wishing Ian happy birthday was finding Ema.

He scanned the crowd and spotted her immediately—impossible to miss in a deep blue gown with intricate black lace that looked like it had been stolen from a Victorian drama. Rus stood beside her like a wolfhound guarding a princess, and Van loomed behind them both like a gothic gargoyle carved from stone and suspicion.

Jay approached quickly. "Ema."

"Jay!" Ema beamed, spinning to show off the dress. "Look at this! Isn't it amazing?"

"It's very dramatic."

"It's beautiful," Ema corrected. She gestured to Lam, who wore a similarly theatrical gown in rose with velvet accents. "We look like we stepped out of a period film."

"You do," Jay admitted. The dresses were glamorous, absurdly over the top, and—to his immense relief—perfectly modest. High necklines, long sleeves, full skirts. Leo had actually kept his promise.

"Leo's stylist said we needed something special. Something memorable. She showed us these and we fell in love immediately." Ema spun, the skirt flowing dramatically. "I feel like a princess in a historical romance."

"You look like one too." Jay turned to Rus. "Thank you for watching them."

"No problem. Van and I have it covered. No one's getting near them without going through us first."

"I appreciate that."

The girls launched into an excited breakdown of their entire day—shopping, makeup, hair, the car ride, the moment they walked into the venue and realized how enormous the party was. Jay listened, half-relieved and half-exhausted, until he could politely extract himself.

"I need to find Leo," Jay said.

"Good luck," Rus said.

Jay found Leo near the bar, sipping champagne and looking thoroughly pleased with himself.

"Leo," Jay said.

"Jay," Leo said warmly. "You look fantastic."

"You said a hundred people. Close friends. Small gathering."

"Did I?"

"Yes."

"Hm. Must have miscounted."

"There are at least five hundred people here. This isn't a party. It's a gala."

"It's a celebration," Leo corrected.

"There are politicians here. Clients. I saw someone from the finance ministry."

"Ian knows a lot of people."

"This is ridiculous. You said small. You said intimate. You said—"

"Look around," Leo interrupted, gesturing to the room.

Jay stopped talking and actually looked.

The venue was massive—chandeliers dripping with crystal, floral arrangements that must've cost thousands, a string quartet playing near the far wall. The guest list was drawn from every tier of power and influence. Politicians, business leaders, old money families. But also artists, writers, people in extravagant clothing who clearly didn't care what anyone thought.

A surprising number of people were unabashedly, flamboyantly queer—men in glittering jackets, women in sharp suits, couples holding hands.

Jay had expected something elegant and restrained. Instead, this was Ian's actual world—enormous, distinguished, glittering. Unapologetic.

Jay's nerves spiked. He was so far out of his depth here. These were people who belonged in spaces like this, who moved through such events with natural grace. And Jay was just someone who'd needed a clothing clerk to tell him how to dress appropriately.

Then he stopped himself. This was Ian's world. Of course it looked like this. Of course Ian moved in circles that were massive and complicated and full of people who lived lives Jay couldn't even imagine. That didn't mean Jay didn't belong here. It meant he had to live up to the occasion.

He took a breath and turned back to Leo. "Fine."

Leo smiled. "See? Not so bad."

"It's terrifying," Jay said.

"You'll be fine."

Jay found Ian by the buffet table, momentarily alone. He approached quickly, before someone else could claim Ian's attention.

"Ian, I'm sorry," Jay said.

Ian looked at him, surprised. "For what?"

"For pushing the party idea. I didn't realize it would be this big. This formal. If I'd known—"

"Jay, stop."

"But you're tense. I can see it."

"I'm always tense at these things. But it's fine. I'm used to it. This isn't so different from charity galas or industry events. Just with more people I actually like."

"You're sure?"

"I'm sure."

Before Jay could respond, a group of guests approached—older men, clearly friends from Ian's past. They swept Ian into conversation, and Jay stepped back, letting him go.

Jay drifted between circles, checking on Ema every fifteen minutes, talking with his coworkers. The team had scattered throughout the venue, each finding their own corner. He found Rin clinging to Luc like a lifeline near the edge of the dance floor.

"You okay?" Jay asked.

"Too many people," Rin muttered.

"You'll survive," Luc said cheerfully. "Just breathe."

Ela and Kim were on the dance floor, laughing and spinning. Even Ray was there, standing stiffly near the wall with a glass of wine, looking like someone had forced him to attend at gunpoint. Jay suspected Leo might actually have threatened him. It was the only explanation.

Max, Amy, and Liv had brought their spouses. Max's wife wore loads of jewellery and laughed as loudly as Max did. Amy's husband looked serious and vaguely unhappy to be there. Liv's husband surprised Jay—long hair, multiple piercings, an air of mild boredom. They stood in a group of six, chatting animatedly.

Zoe stood in a corner chatting with a group of people who were aggressively, gloriously flamboyant—sequins, bold makeup. She looked completely at ease. Tia stood near the bar with a glass of whiskey, looking slightly awkward.

"Tia," Jay said.

"Jay," she said, relieved. "Thank god. I don't know anyone here."

"You know me," Jay said. "Come on."

He led Tia over to where Rus and Van stood guard over the girls. Ema and Lam were still chattering excitedly, but Rus and Van looked like sentries on duty.

"Tia, this is Rus and Van. They're friends. You all have military backgrounds. You'll get along."

Tia's expression brightened immediately. "You were in the service?"

They launched into conversation immediately, trading stories about bases and deployments and the particular insanity of military logistics. Tia's awkwardness vanished within minutes.

Jay turned back to the dance floor and froze.

Luc had Ema on the dance floor, spinning her around, laughing. Mic was entertaining Lam nearby, gesturing wildly while she giggled. Jay shot them both pointed glances.

Rus appeared at his side. "Relax. Luc's harmless. And Mic might be a menace, but he wouldn't go after little girls."

"You're sure?"

"Positive. They're just having fun."

Jay watched for another moment. Rus was right. Luc was being perfectly appropriate, just friendly. And Mic, despite his reputation, kept a respectful distance from Lam.

Still, Jay didn't relax. 

The party was loud, lively, full of laughter and music. People danced. People talked. People drank champagne and ate from the elaborate buffet. And Jay moved through it all, keeping track of everyone, making sure Ema and Lam were safe, checking that the team was comfortable, watching Ian from across the room whenever he could.

He didn't relax for a second. He couldn't shake the feeling that if something went wrong, it would somehow be his fault.

* * *

#### Not boyfriend

At ten, the lights dimmed. A hush fell over the crowd as staff wheeled out the cake.

It was monumental. Excessive. Four tiers of dark chocolate, decorated with sugar flowers that looked too delicate to be real. The crowd pressed closer, phones appearing to capture the moment.

Then the fireworks started.

Jay hadn't expected fireworks. Nobody had mentioned fireworks. But suddenly the patio doors were thrown open, and the night sky erupted in colour—gold, silver, red, cascading patterns that lit up the entire venue.

The crowd gasped, applauded, cheered. Jay stood frozen, watching the display, feeling the absurdity of it all. This wasn't a birthday party. This was a spectacle.

When the fireworks ended, a figure stepped onto the small stage. Jay recognized them immediately—everyone did. A vaudeville star, legendary in certain circles, someone whose name appeared in theater programs and cultural retrospectives. They smiled, raised their arms theatrically, and began singing.

"Happy birthday to you..."

Half the guests squealed. Actual squealing. Jay watched in disbelief as grown adults reacted like they'd just seen their favorite celebrity. Which, he supposed, they had.

The song ended to thunderous applause. Then the stage lights came up fully, revealing an entire orchestra—not the small string quartet from earlier, but a full ensemble. They launched into a sweeping, theatrical piece, and the crowd erupted again.

Ian stood near the center of the patio, looking uncomfortable and vaguely trapped, but when the song ended, he smiled and raised his glass in acknowledgement.

Ray escaped shortly after the cake. Jay spotted him slipping toward the exit, relief visible on his face even from across the room. Jay didn't blame him. He was half-tempted to flee himself.

But he had responsibilities. Ema, the team, Ian, so he stayed.

As the night continued, the atmosphere loosened. Couples drifted into corners, old friends reunited with animated conversation, the music swelled and changed to something more lively. 

Jay checked on Ian repeatedly. Each time he approached, Ian's expression brightened—just slightly, just enough for Jay to notice.

"How are you holding up?" Jay asked during one check-in.

"Surviving."

"That's all we can ask."

Ian almost smiled. "How's Ema?"

"Thriving. I think she's having the time of her life."

"Good. That's good."

Someone called Ian's name from across the room, and he was pulled away again. Jay watched him go, then returned to his orbit—checking guests, monitoring the atmosphere, ensuring everything ran smoothly.

At eleven-thirty, Jay was near a group of Ian's old friends—elegant people in their forties and fifties, holding wine glasses. One of them spotted Jay and smiled. "You must be Jay."

"I am."

"Ian's told us about you. Though he didn't mention how spectacular you are. Your new boyfriend is very impressive, Ian."

Jay went scarlet. "I'm not—we're not—"

"Jay is my assistant. Not my boyfriend."

The friend laughed, delighted. "Oh, my apologies. Well then, are you available? Because if Ian's foolish enough not to claim you, I'd be delighted to—"

Jay felt his face burn hotter. "I'm absolutely not."

The words came out more forcefully than he intended. The friend raised his hands in mock surrender, still smiling. "Fair enough. Can't blame me for asking."

Ian wanted to excuse himself. He wanted to pull Jay into some secluded corner and kiss him breathless. He wanted to do a hundred things he'd never be allowed to do. The thought sent a sharp pain through his entire body.

Around midnight, Jay spotted Eli in the crowd. He was looking thrilled to be there, and when he saw Jay, he immediately approached.

"Jay! This is incredible. Thank you so much for the invitation."

"I'm glad you like it."

"Are you kidding? This is the event of the season. Everyone's talking about it." Eli gestured to the room. "The orchestra, the fireworks, the guest list—it's spectacular."

"Leo went all out."

"He certainly did. I should thank your employer. For allowing me to attend."

"Come on, I'll introduce you."

Jay found Ian near the bar and brought Eli over. "Ian, this is Eli. He's the stylist who helped with my outfit tonight."

Ian extended his hand. "A pleasure. You did excellent work."

Eli shook his hand, looking slightly starstruck. "Thank you, Mr. Hagen. It was my honor. Jay was already a perfect canvas—I just helped him shine."

"You succeeded admirably."

Eli practically glowed. "If you ever need styling services, please don't hesitate to reach out. I'd be delighted to assist."

"I'll keep that in mind."

He departed with a final nod to Jay.

"The acquaintance you mentioned?" Ian asked.

"Yes. He's helped me multiple times with styling advice. I wanted to thank him."

"By bringing him to my birthday party."

"Is that alright? I know I should have asked more specifically—"

"It's fine. You're thoughtful about the people who help you. That's characteristic."

Before Jay could respond, a woman in an expensive gown approached—apparently a city councillor who wanted to discuss potential new projects. Ian was pulled back into conversation, and Jay stepped away to give them space.

Across the room, Ema and Lam had found a table full of flamboyant men who were absolutely delighted by their Victorian gowns. Jay watched as they launched into an animated discussion about makeup techniques, historical fashion, and the best ways to achieve dramatic effects.

The men were patient, enthusiastic, treating the girls like fellow enthusiasts. Ema was asking questions, showing photos on her phone, absorbing every piece of advice.

Jay checked with Rus, who was still standing guard nearby.

"They're fine. Theatre people, I think. They're having a blast."

"As long as they're safe."

"They're safe. Stop worrying."

Jay tried to take the advice. He really did.

The night continued, endless and exhausting. Jay kept orbiting Ian, drawn back to him again and again even as the crowd kept them apart. Each time their eyes met across the room, something passed between them—acknowledgment, reassurance, connection.

They barely spoke. There was no time, too many guests demanding Ian's attention. But Jay felt the pull anyway, the invisible thread that kept bringing him back.

Ian noticed the orbits. Jay could tell by the way Ian's gaze tracked him, by the way Ian's expression eased slightly whenever Jay came into view. Like Jay mattered. Like it made the night slightly more bearable.

At two in the morning, the party was still going, though the crowd had thinned. Jay finally gathered Ema and Lam, who were exhausted but exhilarated.

"That was the best night of my life," Ema said, leaning heavily on Jay as they walked to the car.

"I'm glad."

"Everyone was so nice. And the dresses, and the dancing, and those men who talked about makeup—Jay, it was perfect."

"I'm happy you enjoyed it."

Lam was quieter, but her face glowed. "Thank you for bringing us."

"Of course."

During the taxi ride home Ema and Lam dozed against each other in the back seat. Jay stared out the window. They'd survived. The party had been a success. Ema was happy. Nobody had been hurt or disappointed.

Back at his apartment, the girls stumbled to the bedroom with mumbled goodnight. He texted his mother: "Evening went perfectly. Everyone behaved. The girls had an amazing time. We're home safe."

He removed his outfit, hanging everything carelessly, too tired to do more than basic care, then collapsed onto the couch, bone-tired but still wired from the evening.

* * *

#### You deserve every good thing

Ian arrived home at four in the morning, drained and irritated.

The party had been too much. The attention suffocating. The splendour absurd. Five hundred people wishing him well, wanting his time. The cake, the fireworks, the orchestra—all of it excessive beyond reason.

But Jay… Jay had looked beautiful. Devastating. Walking down those stairs like something out of a dream.

Jay had kept coming back to him, checking on him, seeking him out even in the crush of the crowd. Jay had rejected everyone else who approached him, every subtle and not-so-subtle advance deflected with a simple, definitive no.

Ian dropped his keys on the counter and looked around his living room.

The gifts were already there, arranged by the staff Leo had hired to handle the logistics. Elegant boxes, wrapped packages, things chosen by assistants or purchased out of obligation. Ian ignored all of them and went straight to the one that mattered. The silver-and-gold package with the red ribbon. Jay's gift. The ribbons Jay had chosen made it distinctive among the more elaborate presentations. Ian took it to his kitchen table and sat, just looking at it for a long moment.

Then he unwrapped it, preserving the ribbons, revealing a beautiful wooden box beneath. He opened it to find three crystal bottles and a handwritten note. Ian picked it up and read.

*Ian,*

*I made these for you. Each one for a different moment:*

*The first—citrus, ginger, cardamom. For cold evenings when you need warmth.*

*The second—alpine herbs, gentian, chamomile. For when you're tired and need clarity.*

*The third—red currant, cinnamon, honey. This one reminded me of my childhood. My grandfather made something similar. I hope it brings you comfort.* 

*Happy birthday. You deserve every good thing.*

*—Jay*

Ian read it twice, then a third time, tracing the handwriting with his eyes.

Each one tailored to him. Each one infused with meaning. He picked up the bottle of red currant tincture and opened it. He poured a small amount into a glass and took a sip.

It was sweet and warm, just like Jay. Rich with spice and fruit, smooth on the tongue.

Ian closed his eyes and let the warmth spread through him. It probably tastes like his lips, Ian thought, and the thought hit him with such sharp longing that he had to set the glass down. 

He fell asleep on the couch, still holding the note, glowing despite the exhaustion. The evening wasn't so bad after all.

Ian slept in the next day. He woke at noon to someone knocking persistently on his door. Ian dragged himself upright, disoriented and annoyed, and went to answer it. Leo stood in the hallway.

"Good morning, sunshine."

"What do you want?"

"Just checking on the birthday boy. Making sure you survived the festivities."

"I'm fine. Go away."

"Let me in. I brought coffee."

Ian considered closing the door in his face. But Leo was already pushing past him, carrying two cups from the café down the street.

"You look terrible," Leo said cheerfully, handing him a cup.

"I was asleep."

"At noon? That's not like you."

"I got home at four."

"Fair enough. So. What did you think?"

"It was excessive."

"It was great."

"It was too much, Leo. Five hundred people—"

"Who all care about you. Who all wanted to celebrate you. Stop being ungrateful."

"Fine. It was impressive. Thank you."

"Better. Now, let me try one of Jay's tinctures."

"No."

"Come on. Just a taste."

"Absolutely not."

"Ian—"

"No. They're mine."

Leo laughed, delighted. "You're being territorial. How charming."

"They're a gift. I'm not sharing."

"Fine, fine. But you should know—Jay made those with so much care. Months of trial and error. He turned his entire apartment into a laboratory. Probably three dozen batches before he settled on the final versions. You should have seen him guarding those jars like a dragon protecting treasure. He wouldn't let me sample anything. It was adorable."

"He didn't need to go to that much effort."

"Of course he did. He adores you."

"Leo—"

"You know, I'm surprised you two haven't consummated your obvious union yet."

"Get out," Ian said.

"I'm just saying—"

"Leo."

"Fine, fine. Though really, Ian. The way he looks at you. The way you look at him. It's rather remarkable that you've both maintained this facade."

Ian physically moved Leo toward the door. "Out."

Leo left clearly pleased with himself.

Ian stood in the quiet afterwards, holding the bottle Jay had made for him, feeling undeserving and overwhelmingly lucky.

Leo's unsavoury comments about consummation were ridiculous—Jay's intentions were almost certainly not that carnal. But the sweetness was there regardless. The gentle, tender care that made Ian's chest constrict painfully. He set the bottle down and wondered what he'd done to deserve someone like Jay in his life.

Jay woke up Sunday morning to the sound of the girls already awake and eager to explore.

"Can we go shopping?" Ema asked the moment Jay opened his eyes.

"It's eight in the morning," Jay said.

"Exactly. The stores will be open soon."

Jay sighed and made coffee.

They went shopping. Ema and Lam dragged him through half a dozen stores, exclaiming over clothes and makeup and accessories they couldn't afford but loved looking at. Jay bought them each something small. 

By early afternoon, Leo's driver arrived to take them home. The girls hugged Jay tightly, still buzzing with excitement.

"Thank you, Jay. This was the best weekend of my life."

"You're welcome."

"Will you visit us soon?"

"I'll try."

They climbed into the car, waving through the window as they pulled away. Jay watched them go, feeling exhausted. The weekend was over. The party was done. Now he had to face the aftermath.

He walked home slowly, thinking about Ian. The evening had been much more stressful than he'd thought. The scale, the crowd, the noise—it had been ridiculous. He couldn't shake the feeling that he'd done something wrong.

He'd wanted to bring joy into Ian's life. Instead, he'd brought a spectacle. He'd pushed Ian into the centre of attention, exactly where Ian didn't want to be. He'd turned a simple birthday into a massive, exhausting production. He was sure Ian was unhappy about that. Jay closed his eyes and sighed deeply. He'd have to make it up to him somehow. Apologize. Do something to fix whatever damage he'd caused.

### Chapter 44: Afterglow

#### Early darkness

The fluorescent lights felt particularly harsh on Monday morning. Jay arrived at his desk skittish, bracing himself for whatever reaction Ian might have to the party. The weekend had been a spectacular excess, and now here he was again, surrounded by monitors and spreadsheets and the ordinary machinery of work.

Ian appeared at eight and walked past Jay's desk without stopping.

"Good morning."

"Good morning," Jay said.

That was all. Ian disappeared into his office, and Jay turned back to his screen, uncertain. He couldn’t tell whether Ian seemed annoyed or if he was projecting his own doubts onto him.

The morning workload was unusually heavy. Client requests needed immediate attention, and two international calls were scheduled before ten. The morning briefing was brief—squeezed between Ian's first and second call—and consisted mostly of Jay rattling off urgent items.
