When they were done Jay left the office and returned to his desk. Ian looked exhausted. He was trying to hide it, but Jay could see the weariness, the strain. He wanted to talk to Ian. To explain, to apologize, to make amends for the spectacle of the party, but the workday consumed them both.

By noon, Jay had barely spoken to Ian again. The international calls ran long, and then there was an emergency meeting about a client complaint. At lunch, Jay joined the team at a nearby restaurant. The party was the main topic immediately.

"I'm telling you, the fireworks were overkill, but in the best possible way. Leo doesn't do anything by halves," Luc said. 

"The cake was incredible. I didn't even know they made cakes that tall," Ela added.

"Did you see the ice sculpture? It was an actual phoenix. How do you even carve that?" Kim asked.

Amy smiled. "My husband even admitted he enjoyed it. That's saying something."

"You looked good, Jay. Where did you even get that outfit? It was stunning," Liv asked.

"Thanks, a stylist helped me."

"Well, they knew what they were doing."

The conversation moved on, dissecting other details of the party—the orchestra, the guest list. Everyone agreed it had been a success. Jay listened and nodded and tried to feel reassured by that, but his thoughts kept returning to Ian's expression when the cake had been wheeled out. The way he'd stood there surrounded by five hundred people and looked miserable.

"You alright?" Luc asked.

Jay realized he'd been staring at his untouched sandwich. "Yeah. Just tired."

"It was a long weekend. I'm still recovering too."

The afternoon dragged. Ian's calls continued, one bleeding into the next, and Jay worked through spreadsheets and reports, trying not to watch the clock. By six, most of the office had cleared out. By seven, it was just him and Ian left on the floor.

Finally Jay got up and walked to Ian's office. The door was open. Ian was at his desk, typing something frantically. Jay knocked lightly on the door frame.

Ian looked up. "Yes?"

"Do you need anything else tonight?"

"No, I'm nearly finished here. You can head out if you'd like."

"Actually, I wanted to apologize."

"For what?"

"For the party. I should have pushed back more when Leo started planning everything. I didn't realize how out of hand it would get."

"Leo doesn't take well to being pushed back."

"I know, but still. Five hundred people and fireworks and a vaudeville star—it was too much. I'm sorry. I just—it got out of hand. I know you hate those kinds of events, and Leo turned it into a circus, and I should have—"

"You didn't force me to attend my own birthday party."

"No, but I encouraged it. And now you're stuck with—I don't know, a mountain of gifts you didn't want and probably a headache that hasn't gone away yet."

"Jay. I agreed to the party. I knew what Leo would do with it. You are not responsible for his excess."

"I wanted you to enjoy it. But you just looked tired."

"I was tired. Though I'll admit the aftermath is proving worse than the event itself."

"Aftermath?"

"My living room is now a warehouse. Gifts. Everywhere. I can't walk through it without tripping over something."

"You haven't unpacked them?"

"I opened yours. The rest can wait. Indefinitely, if I'm honest. The entire prospect depresses me."

"I can help with that. Next weekend isn't too busy for me. I can come over and sort everything out. Catalogue it, write thank-you notes if you want, whatever you need."

"You don't have to do that."

"I want to. It's my fault you're buried in gifts anyway."

"It's Leo's fault," Ian corrected, but he didn't argue further. "If you're offering, I won't refuse. Saturday afternoon?"

"That works."

"Good. Thank you, Jay."

"Of course."

Jay left the office feeling better. It wasn't much, but at least he could fix this one thing.

Tuesday's run started in the early evening, just as the sun was setting. The air was even cooler than it had been the week before, autumn asserting itself properly now. Jay pulled his jacket tighter as they started along the river path, and by the time they hit the halfway point, the light was almost gone.

They stopped near the water, both breathing hard. The air in front of them turned to fog with every exhale. Ian bent forward slightly, hands on his knees, and then straightened.

"It's getting dark earlier," Ian said.

"And colder. The weather was much more pleasant this time last year," Jay added.

Ian looked out at the river, where the last traces of daylight were fading. "We should probably pause these until spring. Running in the dark isn't ideal, and the temperature's only going to drop from here."

"Yeah, that makes sense."

"Reporting season starts soon anyway. We won't have the time."

"True."

They stood there for a moment longer, neither moving. The tradition had only been going for a few weeks, but it had become something Jay looked forward to. A reason to spend time with Ian outside the office that didn't require a business excuse.

"Spring, then," Jay said finally.

"Spring," Ian agreed.

When yhey walked back, the cold was biting at their exposed skin.

Thursday afternoon, Jay's phone rang with the car salesman's number.

"I'm still working on finding the right vehicle for your budget and specifications. I'll need another week, maybe a bit more."

"How much more?"

"Hard to say. The market's been tight lately, but I'll keep looking. I haven't let Mr. Hagen down yet, and I don't plan to start now."

"Alright. Thank you."

He hung up and stared at his calendar. Tim's birthday was in mid-October, and the quarter ended soon. Once reporting season hit, Jay's schedule would be a nightmare. He needed this sorted before then, but there wasn't much he could do except wait and hope the salesman's track record held.

Friday evening, Ian appeared at Jay's desk at seven o'clock.

"Ready?" he asked, as if there'd been any question about whether they'd have their weekly dinner.

"Yeah. Where are we going?" 

"That place near the canal." 

"The one with the brick oven?" 

"That's the one."

They didn't discuss further. The Friday dinners had resumed without needing to be renegotiated, slipping back into place as though the party weekend interruption never happened. Ian drove them to the restaurant with wooden tables and a menu that leaned toward game.

They ordered drinks first, and then Ian asked about the car search. Jay explained the delay, and Ian frowned slightly.

"He's reliable. If he says he needs more time, it's because he's being thorough."

"I know. I just—Tim's birthday is coming up, and I wanted to have it sorted before reporting season starts."

"You'll have it sorted. Even if it cuts close."

Jay nodded, trying to believe that.

They talked about work, the upcoming reports. Ian mentioned that Leo was already making noises about the annual gala, which wasn't until January but apparently required months of planning.

When the food arrived, Jay looked down at his plate with mild apprehension. He'd ordered something the waiter recommended, rabbit stew with juniper and chestnuts. The first bite was stronger than he'd expected, rich and almost too gamey, the flavor violently hitting the back of his throat.

He chewed and swallowed, trying not to grimace.

Ian was watching him, and the corner of his mouth lifted. "How is it?"

"It's... interesting."

"You hate it."

"I don't hate it."

"You don't have to finish it."

"I'm not going to waste it."

"Jay, it's food, not a moral failing."

Jay took another bite, forcing himself to chew and swallow. The taste didn't improve and the texture was dense, but he wasn't about to leave half a meal on his plate. Ian was definitely amused now, which only made Jay more determined to finish.

By the time the plate was empty, Jay felt slightly queasy, but at least it was done. Ian was still smiling, the faint kind of smile that softened his whole face.

"You're stubborn," Ian said.

"I don't like wasting things."

"Even when they taste like that?"

"Even then."

Ian shook his head, but he still looked amused. They finished their drinks and left, stepping out into the cool evening air. Ian drove Jay home, and they confirmed their plans for Saturday as the car idled outside Jay's building.

"Two o'clock?" Ian asked.

"I'll be there."

"Good. Thank you again for doing this."

"It's not a problem. Really."

He got out of the car and walked to the entrance, glancing back once to see Ian still waiting until he was inside. Jay waved, and Ian pulled away.

* * *

#### Thoughtful gifts

Jay arrived at Ian's building at exactly two o'clock. The lobby was as imposing as ever. The doorman recognized him now and waved him through without question, but Jay still felt discomfort every time he walked through those doors. This wasn't his world, no matter how many times Ian insisted otherwise.

Ian answered the door almost immediately, dressed in dark trousers and a plain sweater, more casual than Jay expected.

"Come in. Fair warning, it's worse than I described."

Jay stepped inside and stopped. The living room was unrecognizable. Wrapped boxes were stacked everywhere, covering the couch, the coffee table, even parts of the floor. Ribbons and bows and elegant wrapping paper in every conceivable colour.

"Oh," Jay said.

"Yes. Oh. I've been walking around them all week. It's humiliating."

"It's not that bad."

"It's a monument to wealth and excess. People trying to impress me with expensive nonsense I don't want or need."

"We'll sort it out."

"Thank you. I don't think I could've faced this alone."

"We need a system. Sorting first, then unwrapping, then logging who gave what so you can send thank-you notes."

Ian looked at the mountain of presents, then at Jay. "That's going to take hours."

"Then we'd better start."

They set up on the floor, clearing a space in the centre of the room. Ian passed boxes over, while Jay organized them into rough categories by size. It felt strangely intimate—sitting close together on the carpet, surrounded by wrapping paper.

Ian made commentary about each giver. "That's from the Wilsons. They always give crystal. I think they own stock in a glassware company."

Jay unwrapped an elaborate vase. "It's nice crystal, at least."

"It's the fourth vase they've given me."

"What do you do with four vases?"

"Regift them."

They continued. Box after box, gift after gift. Some were thoughtful—uncommon books, quality liquor, things clearly chosen with care. Most were generic—gift baskets, accessories, items selected by assistants, expensive but impersonal. Some were just terrible.

"What is this?" Jay held up a gold-plated pen holder shaped like a swan.

Ian stared at it. "I have no idea."

"It's hideous."

"Put it in the donation pile."

Halfway through the pile, Jay unwrapped a package to find a hoodie with a gym logo and an envelope. He read the card aloud.

"From Rus and the company. It says you need to unwind more. Take your assistant to the sauna and show him how it's done properly. Included: two-person exclusive sauna package voucher. That's—they didn't need to—"

"They're unexpectedly thoughtful. I didn't expect a sauna package."

"I have the same hoodie. I got it at the gym for my one-year anniversary. Now we have matching hoodies."

"That's nice."

Jay set the hoodie aside, still flustered. Matching hoodies. He doubted Ian would ever wear it, but the idea still made his heart race. 

The next gift was a slim envelope, decorated with stickers and hearts. Jay opened it and groaned.

"What is it?" Ian asked.

"From Ema and Lam. Tickets for a spring botanical garden night. In the city where I went to university. They say I can show you around and you'll definitely love it."

Jay felt his face burn. The gift was sweet, and the wishes were cheesy and enthusiastic, but it felt mildly inappropriate, like a thing you gave to a couple, not to a boss and his assistant. He glanced at Ian, ready to apologize for his sister's presumption.

Ian was staring at the ticket, his expression unexpectedly bright.

"A botanical garden at night. I've always wanted to go to something like this, but the opportunity never presented itself. If you're comfortable with it, I'd very much like to go."

"Really?"

"It's novel. Different. But only if you're alright with it. I know it's asking you to take time and travel."

"Of course I'm willing. I just didn't think you'd want to."

"I do. Very much."

Ian set the tickets aside in a spot where they wouldn't get lost. Internally, he was praising Ema for engineering such a perfect excuse for a weekend with Jay. The girl was a genius. This was even better than the sauna voucher from Rus.

The next box was ornate, wrapped in gold paper with an absurdly large bow.

"Oh my god," Jay said.

Inside, nestled in layers of velvet, was an antique brooch. It was enormous, the size of Jay's palm, with intricate gold filigree and a sapphire in the centre so large it looked fake. Except it wasn't fake. It was absolutely, unmistakably real. Gaudy, extravagant, absolutely ridiculous.

"It's from Leo." 

Jay stared at it. Then he started laughing. He couldn't help it. The absurdity of the thing, the sheer ridiculousness of Leo spending two weeks hunting for this monstrosity across multiple countries, all while Jay had been agonizing over whether his homemade tinctures were good enough. He laughed until his sides hurt, collapsing back against the couch.

Ian looked unamused. "It's probably insanely expensive."

"It's hideous."

Jay rolled on the floor, still laughing. "He made me so anxious. I thought my gift was going to look pathetic next to whatever he found, and he comes back with this."

Ian picked up the brooch, examining it critically. "Your gift was better."

Jay sat up, wiping his eyes. "You're just saying that."

"I'm not. Your gift was the best. Well, second best. Right after your sister's gift."

They continued working through the pile, boxes opened, gifts logged, wrapping paper collected into bags. By the time they finished, two empty coffee cups sat on the table, and they were both sprawled on the floor amid the debris.

"I'm sorry this was such a hassle," Jay said.

"You've apologized enough."

"I feel responsible."

"If you truly want to make it up to me, take vacation time without grimacing."

Jay groaned. "Not this again."

"You need rest. You should plan it as penance for the party."

"I'm not going unless you take time off too."

"Spending a week at home is depressing. I don't want to do it."

"Then don't spend it at home."

"I don't have anywhere to go."

They reached a standstill, both stubborn, neither willing to budge. Jay lay on the floor, staring at the ceiling, and felt the comfortable domesticity of the moment—the warmth, the feeling that he could say anything.

"What if—what if I plan a trip, and you come with me? Would you consider taking time off then?"

"You want to travel together?"

"I mean, I'm your assistant. Planning a vacation is practically administrative work."

"That's—I suppose that's reasonable."

"So you'd consider it?"

"I'd reluctantly agree. For your sake. Since you insist."

But Ian's tone was anything but reluctant. He sounded almost eager.

"November? After reporting season ends," Jay proposed.

"That works."

"I'll look for options. Maybe somewhere warm. And with good food."

"I trust your judgement."

They lay there for another moment, both smiling at the ceiling, surrounded by the wreckage of the afternoon. Jay felt absurdly happy. They had the botanical garden trip in spring. They had a vacation in November. So many things to look forward to.

Ian stood and stretched, surveying the now-organized stacks of gifts, and then looked at Jay.

"Stay for dinner. It's the least I can do after making you work all afternoon."

"You didn't make me—"

"Stay anyway."

Jay followed him to the kitchen and sat at the counter, watching Ian move around, chopping vegetables, heating oil, assembling ingredients. The food smelled amazing. Jay's mouth watered as Ian plated two servings and brought them to the table.

"This looks incredible," Jay said.

"Try it before you compliment it."

Jay took a bite. It was delicious.

"You're an amazing cook."

"I'm glad you like it."

It was late by the time Jay left, stepping out into the cool night air. He walked home, already thinking about where they might go in November. Somewhere they could both relax. Somewhere they could spend time together, just the two of them. The thought made him smile all the way home.

* * *

#### Smooth sailing

Jay spent all of Sunday browsing holiday options. He made lists, compared destinations, took notes on activities Ian might enjoy. Places with active ways to spend time—hiking, water sports, cultural sites. Preferably seaside, somewhere warm in November.

And only the absolute top hotels, places Ian would actually want to stay in. Jay looked at the prices and felt his stomach twist, but he pushed the feeling aside. It didn't matter if he had to bankrupt himself. He'd ensure Ian got the best possible experience.

By evening, he had a shortlist of possibilities and a notebook full of details.

Monday was hectic—the last day of September, everyone rushing to finish current tasks before the reporting season began. Jay stayed late, buried in assignments, and barely had time to talk to Ian beyond their morning briefing.

When he finally left at eight, Ian was still at his desk, surrounded by documents. They exchanged a look, Jay hesitating at the door, but there was nothing to say. The quarter was ending. This was normal.

Tuesday, the quarterly reports began.

Day one was preliminary—gathering data, downloading files, sorting through sources. Tedious, time-consuming work that kept everyone at their desks until late evening. Except this time, they finished by four.

Liv and Ray worked like an unstoppable machine. Ray especially—he tore through assignments like a robot, producing virtually flawless work at incredible speed. By the time Jay checked in at three, they were already tackling tasks scheduled for the next day.

Jay stared at his screen, disbelieving. "We're ahead of schedule."

"Way ahead," Luc confirmed, looking equally stunned.

Wednesday was supposed to be the worst day—assembling and reconciling countless rows of numbers, validating data, fixing errors, the boring, grinding work that kept them in the office until midnight. But by three in the afternoon, they were done, moving on to additional assignments with time to spare.

Rin made himself useful, running files between desks, double-checking calculations. Tia adapted quickly, following Rin's lead and staying out of the way when needed.

No overtime. No disasters. No one sleeping in the office.

Ian and Jay almost didn't know what to do with themselves. That evening, they organized tea in the lounge. The office was emptying out—people actually leaving at reasonable hours—and Ian and Jay sat at one of the small tables with cups of tea and Jay's laptop.

"This one has great hiking trails," Jay said, pointing at the screen. "And this one has historical sites nearby. Both are warm in November."

 "What about seaside?"

"All of them are coastal."

"Show me the hotels."

Jay clicked through options. Ian made suggestions, pointing out features he liked—private beaches, good restaurants, proximity to activities.

"This seems expensive," Jay said, hesitating over one particular resort.

"Cost doesn't matter. Leo pays, and you're going as my assistant to coordinate activities. It shouldn't even count as vacation."

Jay couldn't really argue with that. This was exactly how he framed the suggestion, after all. "Alright. I'll look at the top-tier options."

The reports continued their unprecedented smooth progress. Tasks that required weekend work were finished by Friday afternoon. Ray left each day at four, having bludgeoned through enormous portions of work, leaving everyone staring after him in disbelief.

Friday, Ian had an urgent regulatory matter that kept him late. Jay stayed until eight, helping where he could, but eventually had to leave Ian buried in legal documents he couldn't assist with.  At eight, Jay packed his things. He stopped by Ian's office.

"I'm heading out."

"Sorry about dinner."

"It's fine. You have work to do."

"This is going to take all weekend."

"I figured."

They looked at each other for a moment. Jay wanted to say something—hat he'd miss their dinner more than was reasonable—but he just said, "Good night."

"Good night, Jay."

Jay had expected this, prepared for it mentally, but it still left him feeling gloomy as he walked home alone.

Saturday, Jay went to the gym and tried to sweat out the misery. Rus noticed immediately.

"What's got you wound up?"

"Nothing. Just—work stuff."

"Work stuff that has you murdering the rowing machine?"

Jay stopped, breathing hard. "Fine. Your gift. What were you thinking? Take your assistant to the sauna? That's completely inappropriate."

Rus burst out laughing. "Oh my god. You're actually upset about that?"

"It's—it's weird! You can't just—"

"It's hilarious. You're pouting."

"I'm not pouting."

When Rus finally caught his breath, he wiped his eyes. "I'm glad work is less stressful, at least. Seems like Ian took my complaints to heart."

"It's the expanded staff. Liv and Ray are incredible. We're ahead of schedule for the first time ever."

"Good. You deserve a break."

Jay nodded, but the gloom lingered. Work was easier, but that didn't fix the disappointment of the missed Friday dinner.

The following week was equally easy. All emergencies were handled as they appeared, everything proceeding at express speed. The team even resumed their lunch outings—there was no reason not to, given they were on track to finish a day before deadline with no overtime whatsoever.

Jay didn't need to stay late either, but he did. Evenings had become holiday planning time.

On Tuesday, they finalized the details. The Songbird Islands, first half of November. A royal suite in a cliff-side five-star hotel, with a view of the ocean and access to private beaches.

"This is it. Ten days," Jay said. 

"Book it."

Jay pulled out the company card, looking at it like it might bite him. "Aren't we technically defrauding company funds?"

"Leo will survive. He spent millions on that ridiculous brooch. He can endure a couple thousand more for a decent hotel."

"This is more than a couple thousand."

"Book it, Jay."

Jay took a breath and entered the payment information. Ten days. Longer than he'd expected Ian to agree to.

They finished the reports on Wednesday. Everything triple-checked, quadruple-checked, every file polished to perfection. The team sat in stunned silence, looking at their completed work.

"We're done," Amy said, disbelieving.

"Actually done," Max confirmed.

Tia looked impressed. "Is it always like this?"

"No," Luc said immediately. "It's never like this. Normally we're all half-dead by now."

Thursday afternoon, Leo appeared in the office doorway, looking pleased with himself. He clapped his hands together, and the room went quiet.

"Attention, everyone. The promised hot springs trip will take place the last weekend of October. Three days, two nights, all expenses paid. Mark your calendars."

A cheer went through the office. Jay sat at his desk, frozen. His brain supplied an extremely vivid image of Ian in a bathrobe, and he nearly dropped his pen. The intrusive thought made him blush furiously. He ducked his head, pretending to organize papers, hoping no one noticed.

Thankfully, everyone was too busy celebrating to pay attention.

The quarterly party was on Friday. It was held at an elegant venue, perfectly beautiful and tastefully decorated, the atmosphere cheerful and low-stress. Rin and Tia didn't look nervous at all, seasoned now by Ian's birthday extravaganza. Ray was absent—clearly, whatever threats Leo had used before had lost their efficacy.

Jay kept drifting to Ian's side throughout the evening. They talked about work, about the upcoming hot-springs trip. It was comfortable, and yet—

Yet Jay felt the undercurrent of disappointment. The party was nice, but it was the second Friday dinner in a row that didn't happen, and that felt bad. And even though the circumstances were understandable, even though work had gone well, the absence of those evenings still left an ache.

They had so much to look forward to, so many plans, so many promises of time together, but right in this moment, Jay missed the feeling of having Ian's full attention.

Ian was talking to Amy when Jay approached, but he turned immediately.

"Enjoying yourself?" Ian asked.

"It's nice."

"But?"

"Nothing. It's a good party."

Jay had the uncomfortable sense that Ian could see straight through him, but Ian didn't press, just nodded and turned back to the conversation.

They saw each other every day, worked side by side, planned trips together, and yet Jay missed him desperately.

* * *

#### Lovesick

Saturday at the gym was miserable. Jay pushed himself, but his mood dragged behind him like a weight. Rus noticed immediately.

"Still mad about the gift?" Rus asked.

"What? No."

"Then what's wrong?"

"The car salesman is taking forever. Tim's birthday is next weekend and I still don't have the car."

"He'll come through."

"What if he doesn't?"

"He will."

Rus tried to engage him in conversation, but Jay gave monosyllabic answers until Rus finally left him alone.

The rest of the weekend was worse. Jay stayed home, flipping through television channels, scrolling through his phone. He told himself there was nothing wrong. Work was good. The vacation was booked. Everything was fine. The car will be ready on time.

But it didn't feel fine.

Jay was at his desk by eight on Monday, working through emails when his phone rang. The car salesman.

"Mr. Hall, I apologize for the delay, but I've found it. Are you available to come in this week?"

"Yes, absolutely. When?"

"Wednesday afternoon works for me. Say, five thirty?"

"Yes. Thank you."

He hung up and immediately went to Ian's office. 

"The salesman found a car. I'm meeting him Wednesday at five thirty."

"Good. I'll drive you."

 "You don't have to—"

"The reports are done. Workload is low. I don't need to stay late. Might as well confirm my recommended salesman did his job properly."

"Alright. Thank you."

Ian nodded, and Jay returned to his desk.

Wednesday at five, they left the office together. Ian drove them across the city to the dealership. The salesman met them at the entrance, shaking hands with Ian first and then Jay.

"Mr. Hagen, always a pleasure. And Mr. Hall, I think you'll be very pleased with what I found."

He led them to the lot. The car was unassuming, a dark blue sedan from a brand known for reliability and safety. Newer than Jay had hoped for and not too heavily used.

The salesman handed Jay a folder. "Full inspection paperwork. Service history. Everything's in order."

Jay opened the folder and stared at the pages. Technical specifications. Maintenance records. Numbers and terms he barely understood.

"I don't know much about cars," Jay admitted.

Ian took the folder from him and flipped through it. The salesman stood by, waiting. After a moment, Ian handed the folder back to Jay.

"Everything looks in order. It's a solid choice."

"You're sure?"

"The service history is clean and the price is fair. I'd recommend it."

Jay looked at the car again. It wasn't flashy or exciting, but it was reliable and safe.

"Alright. I'll take it."

The salesman smiled. "Excellent. Let's handle the paperwork."

They went inside, and Jay signed what felt like an endless stack of documents. Ian stood beside him, occasionally pointing out a clause or asking the salesman a clarifying question.

The salesman handed him the keys. "Congratulations, Mr. Hall. Drive safe."

They walked back out to the lot. Jay stood beside the car, keys in hand, feeling oddly uncertain.

"Thank you for coming with me," Jay said.

"It wasn't a problem. Drive safely. I'll see you tomorrow."

He walked to his own car, and Jay watched him go, disappointed. The whole thing had taken less than an hour. He'd wanted more time.

Jay got into the car and adjusted the seat, the mirrors, familiarizing himself with the controls. Then he started the engine and drove home. That evening, he called his mother.

"Jay! Did you find something?"

"Yes, I picked it up today. It's a good car. Tim will like it."

"Oh, thank goodness. I was starting to worry. You kept saying everything was fine, but—"

"Everything is fine. It just took longer than expected."

His mother sighed, relief evident in her voice. "You're a good brother, Jay. Tim's going to be so happy."

"I hope so."

She'd called several times over the past weeks, asking about progress. Jay had lied confidently each time, saying everything was proceeding smoothly, hoping the salesman wouldn't disappoint him. They talked for a few more minutes, confirming details for the weekend. Jay promised to arrive Friday evening. 

Jay packed Thursday evening, planning to leave straight from work to beat the traffic. He left at four on Friday, wanting to get a head start. Four hours to his hometown, longer if the Friday traffic was bad.

He stopped by Ian's office before leaving. 

"Heading out?"

"Yes. I'll see you Monday."

"Drive safely. And enjoy the weekend."

"Thank you."

They looked at each other for a long moment. Jay wanted to say something, but he didn't know what. A third Friday dinner lost.

"I'll see you Monday," Jay said again.

"Yes. Monday."

Jay left the office and walked to the parking garage. The custom they'd built felt fragile suddenly, like it was dissolving.

He got into the car and started the engine, pulling out into the Friday evening traffic. The drive ahead was long, but at least he had the car for Tim. He told himself things would get back to normal in November. After the hot springs trip. After the vacation. He just had to wait.

The drive out of the city was slow, traffic exactly as bad as he'd predicted. Jay gripped the steering wheel and tried to focus on the road, on anything except the persistent ache that had taken up residence in his chest. Four hours stretched ahead of him. Four hours to think about everything he was trying not to think about.

He'd never felt like this before. This hollow, aching wrongness. Like something essential was missing, even though objectively everything was fine. He saw Ian every day. They talked, they worked together, they had plans stretching months into the future. So why did it feel like he was starving?

The runs were postponed. The dinners no longer happened. Their morning briefings were short. The evenings planning trips in the office lounge had stopped once the reporting season ended. These little pieces that had given shape to his weeks were gone, and nothing had replaced them. Jay was miserable and didn't know how to cope with this.

He turned on the radio and drove.

* * *

#### Pining

Friday evening, Ian sat alone in his apartment with a glass of Jay's red-currant tincture.

He'd poured just a small amount, savouring the sweet warmth. It tasted like comfort. Like Jay's devotion poured into a tangible liquid. Ian set the glass down and told himself to behave.

This was ridiculous. He was a grown man, forty years old, sitting alone and pining like a teenager. Jay was gone for a weekend. Two days. That was nothing. Ian had spent years alone. He could certainly manage forty-eight hours.

Except it wasn't nothing. They saw each other constantly at work and somehow not at all.

It wasn't any easier for him than it was for Jay, though Jay probably didn't realize that. The missed Friday dinners felt like a loss, something slipping through his fingers that he didn't know how to hold onto. But he had things to look forward to. The hot-springs trip. The vacation in November.

Ian couldn't believe they actually had vacation plans. He couldn't remember the last time he'd taken a real vacation. A decade, maybe more. He'd never thought much about it before. Work had always been enough. But now—now the perspective of that trip was what held him together. Knowing that in a few weeks, he'd have Jay's undivided attention again.

Jay arrived late Friday night, parking the car at the neighbour's house and sneaking into his mother's home like a conspirator. He'd barely closed the door when footsteps thundered down the stairs.

"Jay!"

Ema reached him first, throwing her arms around him. Ari appeared next, and even Tim came down, trying to look casual but clearly excited.

"You made it," Tim said.

"Of course I made it. It's your birthday."

His mother appeared in the hallway. "Come in, come in. Are you hungry? I saved dinner."

"I'm fine. I ate on the way."

"Tea, then. Sit down, tell us about the drive."

Jay let himself be pulled into the warmth of the house, surrounded by voices and the comfort of family.

Saturday afternoon, Tim's birthday party took place in a small local venue with worn floors, a place that hosted birthday parties, anniversary dinners, and graduation celebrations for families that couldn't afford anything fancier. It was cramped, decorated with streamers and balloons, tables crowded together.

Family and friends filled the space with noise. Uncles Jay rarely saw, cousins he didn't recognize, Tim's closest classmates. Ari played with younger children in the corner. Ema had brought Lam, the two of them inseparable these days. Instead of the glamorous Victorian gowns from Ian's gala, they wore simple dresses. They still talked about the ball though, Jay overheard them reminiscing about the orchestra and the fireworks.

The food was adequate. Typical small-town venue dishes, heavy on volume to compensate for any lack of sophistication. Casseroles and roasted vegetables and platters of meat. The cake was big enough to feed everyone twice over, decorated with blue frosting. Music played from a loudspeaker in the corner, slightly too loud and slightly distorted.

Jay found a seat near the back and watched the room. His stepfather's side of the family was here in force, cousins and aunts and uncles who had never been particularly warm toward him. His mother's side was smaller, mostly older relatives who remembered him as a child.

He'd always found these gatherings awkward. The sense of being an outsider in his own family, tolerated but not quite belonging. But today, he discovered he didn't care as much anymore. He had his own life now. This was just one part of it, not the whole.

This was the opposite of Ian's gala. Shabby around the edges, messy, imperfect, nothing elegant about it. But it was full of love.

Jay was reminded that he came from humble beginnings. This was his world—simple, warm, unpretentious. He didn't need to perform here or worry about wearing the right clothes.

When the time came for the big reveal, Jay's mother called for everyone's attention. Tim stood in the centre of the room, looking confused.

"What's going on?"

Jay pulled the car keys from his pocket. "Happy birthday."

"What?"

"Come outside."

The whole party followed them out to the parking lot, where the blue sedan was waiting. Tim stopped dead, his mouth falling open.

"Is that—"

"It's yours. Happy eighteenth."

Tim made a sound that was half-laugh, half-shout, and threw his arms around Jay. "Are you serious? This is mine?"

"Yes."

"Oh my god. Oh my god." Tim pulled back and ran to the car, circling it like he couldn't believe it was real. "This is insane. Jay, this is—I can't—"

Tim got into the driver's seat and immediately started adjusting everything, his hands shaking with excitement. Jay's stepfather came over and clapped Jay on the shoulder, the first real acknowledgement he'd given him all day.

"It's a good thing you did."

Jay nodded and watched. His mother was crying. Ema was filming the whole thing on her phone.

Later, when people were starting to leave, Jay found Ema sitting outside on the venue's small patio. Jay sat down beside her. "I wanted to ask you something."

"Sure."

"The botanical garden tickets. For Ian's birthday. Why did you choose that?"

"Leo helped me pick it. He said Ian would definitely love a change of scenery."

Of course. Of course it was Leo's scheme. Everything always was. But he wasn't really mad anymore. If anything, he was pleased. They had something to look forward to in spring. A whole weekend away from the city, just the two of them.

"Did he tell you anything else?"

"Just that Ian never goes anywhere fun, and you'd probably enjoy showing him around your old city. Was it okay? I wasn't sure if it was too personal."

"No, it was a good choice. He's excited about it."

"Good. I'm glad."

"Thanks, Ema," Jay said.

She bumped her shoulder against his. "Anytime."

Sunday afternoon, Jay took the train back to the city. He hadn't traveled by train in years, not since he'd started working with Ian. Before that, trains had been his primary method of getting anywhere. The process felt nostalgic if slightly uncomfortable, requiring him to change trains twice, first in the nearby city, then in a bigger hub along the way. Each transfer meant waiting on platforms, watching the crowds, hauling his bag from one train to another. It was tedious, much longer than driving would have been, but he'd left the car with Tim.

He sat by the window and watched small towns give way to suburbs, then to the outskirts of the city. By the time he reached his stop, evening was falling. He took a taxi to his building, and when he reached his apartment, he pulled out his phone and sent a text to Ian. "Everything went great. Home safe."

Ian's reply came within minutes: "I'm glad. Get some rest. See you tomorrow."

Ian read Jay's text and set his phone down on the coffee table. Home safe. Good. He opened the wooden box again and poured another small measure of the red currant tincture. Just a small amount, like medicine. Medicine for pining.

### Chapter 45: Steam and stars

#### Salt-ocean smell

The autumn air cut through Jay's coat as he stepped out of the metro station Monday morning. The chill was welcome, sharp enough to clear his head. He walked to Northlight Financial Center with his heart doing somersaults in his chest. He'd been gone two days, and he felt ridiculous about how much he wanted to see Ian again.

Jay arrived early, but he didn't go straight to his desk. Instead he walked to the window and looked down at the street below, scanning the morning traffic for Ian's car. He stayed there, watching, aware he was being absurd but unable to help himself.

A silver car turned the corner.

Jay straightened. The car pulled into the underground parking entrance and disappeared from view. He counted the time it would take to park, walk to the elevator, ride up. His pulse quickened.

Ian walked in, and their eyes met. They both smiled, each trying to conceal the relief of seeing the other, but the joy was impossible to mask despite their best attempts.

"Good morning," Ian said, crossing the space between them.

"Morning," Jay replied, trying to sound casual and failing completely.

"How was the trip?"

"Tiring, but good. Tim loved the car. The party was fun. The train ride home was long. I slept most of it."

"You should have taken an extra day to rest."

"No. I mean, I wanted to be back. For work. To prepare for the hot springs trip."

They stood there, both smiling like idiots, until Amy's arrival broke the spell. Then Ian moved toward his office, and Jay turned to his computer.

“A bit early,” she said, setting down her bag. “Wanted to get ahead of everything.”

Jay nodded. “Yeah, good call.”

Soon everyone was hard at work preparing for the upcoming trip. The departure was planned for Friday morning, which meant the team wanted to ensure no backlog would greet them when they returned. Tasks were completed efficiently, everything put in order.

No one worried about falling behind. The atmosphere was light, energized by anticipation. Everyone looked forward to the weekend.

Wednesday afternoon, Jay left went shopping. Warm sweaters, gloves, thick socks. He bought a fluffy hat with earflaps that made him look slightly ridiculous but would definitely keep him warm.

Thursday evening he packed, laying everything out on his bed first. Casual clothes, resort-appropriate items, a suit just in case something formal came up. The warm sweaters went in first, then everything else. He was done in half an hour, no longer agonizing over every choice. Whatever he brought would be adequate.

Friday morning, Ian picked him up after breakfast. Jay waited outside his building, watching Ian's car pull up to the curb.

"Ready?" Ian asked as Jay climbed in.

"Ready."

They drove straight to the airport, arriving to find most of the team already gathering near the departure terminal. Luc was talking animatedly to Rin. Ela and Kim stood together near the entrance. Zoe and Amy were checking their tickets.

Then Ray appeared, and everyone stopped talking. He stood there with a large backpack, surveying the terminal. 

Luc recovered first. "Ray. You're coming?"

"Yes." 

"But you never—you don't do company trips." 

"I love hot springs," Ray said laconically.

The words were delivered so flatly that Jay couldn't tell if he was serious. Apparently no one else could either, judging by the shocked looks being exchanged. Ray loved hot springs? Or had Leo threatened him again?

The flight to Ashland took two hours. Boarding and disembarking took longer than the flight itself—the shuffle of luggage, passengers, security checks. 

Jay sat by the window, watching the landscape beneath them. Ian sat beside him, reading something on his tablet. Every so often Jay would glance over. Ian would look back, smile slightly, and return to his reading.

The plane descended smoothly. They landed with barely a bump, and then everyone was gathering bags and filing out into the terminal into air so fresh and cold it stung. Jay tugged on his new hat immediately.

"Nice hat," Ian said.

"It's warm."

"It has earflaps."

"I know what it has."

Ian's mouth twitched, but he didn't say anything else.

A bus waited outside the airport to transport them to their accommodations. The drive took forty minutes along winding coastal roads, the ocean visible through breaks in the rocky landscape. The air smelled like salt and stone.

Finally, the resort appeared, a sprawling modern structure of glass and wood built into the cliff side. It was small, visibly exclusive, with only a few other guests walking the grounds. Everything looked pristine and sophisticated. The ocean roared below. The wind was fierce here, whipping hair and coats as they climbed out. 

A hostess greeted them in the lobby.

"Welcome to Frostbay Sanctuary. You'll be staying in our two-person suites, each with a private balcony and ocean view. Mr. Turner has arranged everything. If you'll follow me, I'll show you to your rooms, and then we'll go over the facilities and schedule."

She distributed key cards—Rin with Ray, which made Rin's face light up with barely contained joy. Luc with Max. Liv with Tia. Ela and Kim together as usual. Zoe with Amy. And Ian with Jay.

No one complained about the arrangements. The resort was incredibly beautiful, everything fresh and meticulously maintained, the air carrying that persistent salt-ocean smell that made everything feel clean.

They were shown to their rooms—spacious, modern, with minimalist furniture and stone accents. The bathroom featured a high-tech spa tub with more settings than Jay could immediately parse.

After lunch in the resort's dining room—fresh seafood and local produce prepared simply—they changed into swimsuits in their suite, wrapping themselves in the enormous white bathrobes provided by the resort. They headed down to the pool area, following the signs toward the largest soaking pool. The hostess met them there and launched into an explanation of the facility.

"The water is rich in minerals and very good for the skin, though I recommend keeping your hair dry if possible. The clay at the bottom has excellent health benefits. Feel free to apply it anywhere. The pools are quite warm, around forty degrees, so take breaks if you need them. Otherwise, just relax and enjoy."

The pool stretched out before them, its water an impossible turquoise blue. Steam rose from the surface in wisps, dissipating into the cold air. Several smaller pools dotted the area, and further down the cliff Jay could see another pool directly adjacent to the ocean, waves crashing just meters away.

They shed their robes quickly and entered the water to escape the chill. The heat was immediate and intense, sinking into muscles and bones. 

Ray was already in the main pool, stationed in a corner with a contented expression, enthusiastically smearing clay across his arms.

Rin appeared beside Jay, watching Ray with delight. "Did you see? He's actually smiling."

"I don't think that's a smile," Jay said.

"It's close enough."

Rin positioned himself near Ray, copying his clay application with exaggerated movements. Ray glanced at him but said nothing, which Rin apparently took as permission to continue.

Luc floated around the pool, completely disregarding the fact that he was supposed to mind his hair, which was getting progressively wetter and more disheveled.

Jay splashed around for a few minutes, feeling the hot water immediately relax muscles that had been unnaturally tense lately—though he hadn't quite realized how tense until until the warmth began to undo it. The heat worked its way through his shoulders, his back, his legs. 

Finally, he found a spot near Ian, who had chosen a stone seat along the pool's side. Jay sank down beside him, the water deep enough to cover their shoulders. It felt blissful.

They reclined against the smooth stone, the water lapping gently around them, steam rising into the cold air above. Ian's eyes were closed, his expression peaceful.

The heat melted away everything—the missed Friday dinners, the stress of the quarter, the constant low-level anxiety. Here, in the steaming water with the cold air on his face and Ian beside him, none of it mattered. He could stay like this for hours.

* * *

#### Aurora

They soaked for about two hours, taking short breaks to step out into the chilly air for water and to cool down before plunging back into the heat. The heat worked its way deep into Jay's bones, leaving him loose and heavy in the best way. Around him, the team had spread out across the pool, some chatting, others floating in silence.

Eventually Ela climbed out, wrapping herself in her robe. "I think I'm done. Any more and I'll melt completely."

"Agreed," Amy said, following her.

One by one they extracted themselves from the water. Jay felt boneless as he wrapped himself in his robe, the cold air shocking after the pool's heat.

"We should walk before dinner. The views are supposed to be incredible," Zoe said.

"It's freezing," Kim pointed out.

"That's what coats are for."

They returned to their rooms to bundle up properly. Jay layered his warmest sweater under his coat, added his new scarf and gloves, and headed down the path toward the ocean. The wind was cutting through layers, but the landscape was worth it. The sun had already set, leaving the sky a deep indigo streaked with the last traces of orange. Stars were beginning to appear overhead, brighter than they ever were in the city. The horizon stretched endlessly, the island's cliffs rising dark and dramatic against the fading light.

Zoe was excited. "We have to hike tomorrow. Look at this terrain—there are trails everywhere."

"In this cold?" Ela asked, pulling her coat tighter.

"It's great hiking weather. Cold but not too cold. We'll warm up once we start moving."

"I'm in," Luc said immediately.

Ray, walking a few paces behind, spoke up. "There's a route that passes several natural hot springs at the base of the cliffs."

"We're doing that one," Zoe agreed.

They walked for another twenty minutes, boots crunching on stones, until the cold became uncomfortable, then retreated toward the warmth of the main building. Jay's face felt frozen, his fingers numb, but he was smiling.

Dinner was served in a room with a massive fireplace crackling at one end. The table was low to the ground, surrounded by plush pillows instead of chairs. They sat awkwardly at first, trying to find comfortable positions, but the informality suited the mood. Everyone was in casual clothes, even Ian wearing a loose cream sweater.

The food was hearty—thick stews with root vegetables and tender meat, herbal breads still warm from the oven, salads with sharp vinaigrette, small dishes of pickled vegetables and creamy butter. Jay took a piece of bread, dunked it into the stew, bit down and immediately sighed deeply in appreciation. 

"This is incredible."

"It's good," Ian agreed.

Jay wasn't interested in restraint. He sprawled gracelessly on the floor cushion, no longer caring about presentation, and devoted himself entirely to the food. The bread was great for soaking up every drop of stew, and he stuffed his mouth full without apology. Between bites he kept declaring, "This is heaven. Actually heaven."

Ian sat properly upright beside him, watching Jay eat with undisguised fondness. Jay was too absorbed in his dinner to notice, making happy noises over the food. It was unbelievably endearing.

Around them, the team had fallen into their own conversations. Rin was making a valiant attempt to engage Ray, asking about different hot spring minerals and water temperatures. Surprisingly, Ray kept responding, commenting about the pools and tomorrow's planned activities.

"The wild pools have different mineral content than the resort ones. More sulphur."

"Oh, that's interesting. "Do you soak often? Back home, I mean?"

"Sometimes."

It was like watching someone try to befriend a particularly taciturn cat, Jay thought. But Ray seemed more open, perhaps mellowed by hours in hot water.

Tia sat between Luc and Max, all three of them drinking ice-cold vodka from small glasses. Ela watched them with disapproval.

"You're going to regret that tomorrow," she said.

"That's tomorrow's problem," Max replied cheerfully, pouring another round.

Kim laughed. "Let them suffer. It'll be entertaining."

Zoe had her phone out, map pulled up, tracing routes with one finger. "If we leave early we can hit three viewpoints before lunch. The second one overlooks the cliffs on the eastern side."

At the far end of the table, Amy and Liv were discussing something work-related. Max caught wind of it and called out, "No work talk! We're supposed to relax and forget about the office."

Amy waved him off. "We're just talking about process improvements."

"That's still work!"

Jay laughed, taking another piece of bread. "This is amazing. I never want to leave."

Ian smiled at that, sitting straight-backed while Jay sprawled beside him like a contented cat. "You're going to make yourself sick."

"Worth it."

As full darkness fell, the hostess appeared at the door. "If you're interested, the aurora is visible tonight. We have a viewing pool at the ocean's edge—it's a bit of a walk, but the perspective is spectacular."

Everyone agreed immediately and hurried to their rooms to grab bathrobes and towels. Luc and Max were noticeably unsteady, the vodka having done its work, but they insisted they were fine.

"I'm not missing the aurora because I had a few drinks," Luc declared, swaying slightly.

Ray led the group down the lantern-lit path toward the ocean, moving with surprising confidence through the dark. The temperature had dropped further, the wind bitter and unrelenting. Jay huddled into his robe, walking quickly to keep warm.

The pool appeared at the edge of a rocky outcrop, steam rising dramatically into the cold air. It was darker than the main pool, the water appearing almost black in the dim light, and it sat directly adjacent to the ocean. Waves crashed just meters away, spray occasionally misting over the rocks.

They shed their robes and practically dove into the water, gasping with relief as the heat enveloped them. The pool was deep, the bottom smooth stone, the water almost too hot, borderline painful, but after the freezing air it felt incredible.

Above them the aurora unfurled. Green light rippled across the sky in waves, brightening and fading, joined by threads of purple and blue. It moved like it was alive, shimmering and folding. The stars were everywhere, brilliant in the absence of city light.

The pool's lighting was minimal, designed not to interfere with the view, revealing just enough to show the spectacular setting—hot spring water meeting the edge of the ocean.

"Holy shit," Luc whispered.

Jay couldn't speak. He tilted his head back and watched the lights dance. The ocean roared beside them, cold spray drifting over the edge of the pool. He drifted toward the ocean, drawn by the juxtaposition, and reached out experimentally, extending one hand toward the seawater. The cold hit like a shock and he yanked his hand back with a yelp.

Ian chuckled. "What did you expect?"

"I don't know. Not that."

The contrast was absurd. The water they sat in was blisteringly hot, while inches away the ocean was near freezing. The two existed side by side, separated by a thin wall of stone. Above them the aurora continued its display, colours bleeding across the sky. The stars gleamed between the curtains of light. Jay could hear the ocean, smell the salt, feel the steam on his face and the heat surrounding his body. This was the most beautiful thing he'd ever witnessed. Nothing else came close.

They stayed for an hour, watching the lights fade and intensify, fade and intensify. Eventually the hostess returned, reminding them not to overdo it. They climbed out reluctantly, wrapping themselves in the robes and hurrying back up the path. The wind was worse now, vicious and unrelenting. Jay's hair was still wet and the cold made his scalp ache.

Back in their suite, Ian disappeared into the bathroom first, showering quickly. Jay waited his turn, then eyed the enormous spa bath tucked in the corner.

"I'm taking a bath," he announced.

"Don't fall asleep in there."

Jay filled the huge tub, dumping in bath salts until the water turned milky. He climbed in and sank down with a groan. The tub had jets and he turned them all on, fiddling with the settings until the water churned around him. It was absurd. He had a bath at home now, used it regularly, but this was different. The jets pulsed against his back and he grinned stupidly at nothing.

When he finally emerged, wrapped in the resort's plush robe, Ian had opened a bottle of local wine and was reading in one of the chairs by the window.

"There's a glass for you," Ian said, gesturing to the nightstand.

Jay grabbed the wine and collapsed onto his bed, sprawling across the covers without bothering to arrange himself properly. The wine was crisp and cold, perfectly complementing the lingering heat in his muscles from the bath.

"This place is incredible," he announced to the ceiling. 

"Leo has excellent taste when he applies himself," Ian replied.

"The aurora was unreal. Did you see how bright it got near the end? And those colours. The pools are amazing. The food is amazing. The air smells amazing. Even the cold is great."

"You're very enthusiastic tonight."

"I don't think I've been this relaxed in my life."

"I'm glad."

They talked about the wind and the food. Jay's words came slower, his sentences trailing off. The wine and the heat and the long day made eyelids heavy, the comfort of the bed pulling him down. He didn't notice when he fell asleep. One moment he was talking, the next he was gone, glass still half full on the floor beside the bed.

Ian retrieved a blanket from the closet and draped it over him. Jay didn't stir. Ian stood there for a moment, watching him. 

When was the last time Ian had felt this happy? He couldn't remember. Maybe never. Certainly not in recent years. Watching Jay sleep, knowing they had an entire weekend ahead in this beautiful place, filled him with bliss. Not the fleeting kind that came and went, but a bone-deep contentment.

* * *

#### Very interesting wood

Breakfast was lively, Zoe dominating the conversation with her phone in hand, swiping through trail maps and photos of dramatic cliff formations.

"This route goes along the eastern ridge. Incredible views, and there's a natural arch about three kilometres in."

"What about this one? It passes four wild springs," Ray asked, pointing to a different path.

"But the views aren't as good."

"The springs are better."

"We could combine them. Take the ridge path to the arch, then loop back through the spring valley. If we leave now and pack food."

"I'm coming with you," Rin said.

"Me too," Luc said, shoveling eggs into his mouth.

Max shook his head. "Too ambitious for me. I'll take the easy route."

"Same. I'm here to relax, not march through the wilderness," Kim agreed. 

Jay focused on his breakfast, already dreading the cold. The dining room was warm, but he could see the frost on the windows. The weather had dropped even further overnight.

After breakfast they all returned to their rooms to prepare. Jay layered his warmest sweater under his winter coat, added his scarf and fluffy hat and gloves. He decided this should be adequate. The coat had served him well through city winters.

They gathered in the lobby, and Jay immediately felt under-dressed. Zoe wore what looked like mountaineering gear, with technical fabrics and insulated layers. Ray's jacket was similar, designed for serious cold. Even Luc had a proper winter hiking coat, thick and wind-resistant.

"Everyone ready?" Zoe asked.

They stepped outside into air so cold it hurt to breathe. Jay's eyes watered immediately. He pulled his scarf tighter and told himself it would be fine once they started moving.

The trail began gently, leading away from the resort along the cliff edge. The wind was relentless, whipping across the exposed landscape with nothing to stop it. Jay's teeth started chattering within the first ten minutes. His coat was clearly insufficient for this harsh northern weather. He eyed his coworkers' technical jackets with growing distrust of his own choices.

At the first junction, Zoe outlined the plan. "Ray, Rin, Luc and I are taking the longer route to the springs. The rest of you can take this path up the hill—shorter, but the view from the top is supposed to be beautiful."

"Sounds good," Amy agreed.

The groups split, Zoe's team heading off while the rest began the ascent up the smaller hill. The climb wasn't difficult, but the cold was brutal. Jay focused on putting one foot in front of the other, trying to ignore how much he was shaking.

"You okay?" Ian appeared beside him, looking annoyingly comfortable in a sleek dark jacket.

"Fine," Jay managed through chattering teeth.

Ten minutes later, Ian checked again. "Jay?"

"I'm fine."

"Jay, you're turning blue."

"Just cold. It's fine."

Another ten minutes passed. Jay's nose was running and he couldn't feel his toes. Ian stopped walking and turned to face him.

"We're going back."

"What? No. I can keep going."

"You're about to drop dead from hypothermia. We're returning to the resort."

"Ian, I'm fine, really—"

"Jay."

Jay wanted to protest further, but honestly he was miserable. The cold had worked its way deep into his bones, and he couldn't feel his fingers anymore.

"Okay," he admitted.

Ian called ahead to Amy. "We're heading back. Jay's too cold."

"See you at lunch," Amy shouted back.

The walk back seemed longer than the walk out. Jay concentrated on moving, keeping his arms wrapped around himself for whatever warmth he could generate. Ian stayed close beside him. When they finally reached the resort, Jay practically fell through the door.

"Come on," Ian said, steering him toward their suite.

In their room, Jay collapsed into a chair and pulled off his gloves with numb fingers. "I don't understand. Why wasn't anyone else bothered by the freezing cold?"

"Because we wore appropriate gear."

"I was wearing my warmest sweater."

Ian was pulling off his jacket. Underneath he wore a regular sweater, but when he lifted it slightly Jay saw another layer beneath. "Thermal underwear. You need base layers for this kind of weather."

Jay stared at the garment. "Thermal underwear?"

"Yes. You wear it under your regular clothes. It traps body heat."

Of course Ian had known about proper gear. Ian probably owned an entire collection of technical clothing for every conceivable weather condition.

"I didn't know that existed. I thought the sweater would be enough. Next time, tell me I need thermal underwear before I almost die."

"I assumed you knew."

"Why would I know? I'm from a small town in the south. We don't have extreme weather."

"Noted. Next time, I'll provide a full equipment list. Hot spring?"

"Yes. Please. Immediately."

They changed and made their way down to the pool area, this time choosing one of the smaller, secluded pools tucked away from the main complex. Jay descended into the hot water with a groan, the heat immediately working on his frozen muscles.

"This is better. This is so much better."

The pool was peaceful, the sound of waves distant. Jay felt his body slowly returning to normal, warmth replacing the bone-deep chill. By the time they returned to the main building for lunch, Jay had recovered completely. The rest of the team was already there, eating and chatting. Jay slid into his seat and immediately launched into his complaints.

"No one told me I needed thermal underwear. I didn't even know that was a thing people wore."

Kim laughed. "You went hiking in arctic wind without base layers?"

"I had my warmest sweater."

"Oh, Jay. You poor thing," Ela shook her head.

"I even brought heating pads, but I still wouldn't hike as far as Zoe's platoon," Kim added.

Tia agreed. "I had enough discomfort during my military years. I much prefer the pampering activities now. You won't catch me freezing on a mountain."

"Smart," Jay muttered into his soup.

After lunch people split off to explore the resort. Liv, Amy, Ela, and Tia went to book spa treatments. Max and Kim discovered a pool table in a rec room and disappeared immediately.

Ian looked at Jay. "Want to explore?"

"Sure."

They wandered through the resort, discovering various amenities. A library, a meditation room, several smaller pools in addition to the main ones. Eventually they found a terrace overlooking the ocean, equipped with gas outdoor heaters and thick blankets arranged on oversized armchairs.

"This is great," Jay said, claiming a chair near one of the heaters.

Staff appeared with a pot of hot spicy tea and big ceramic cups. Jay wrapped both hands around his cup and pulled a blanket over his legs.

The sun hung low in the sky, beginning its slow descent toward the horizon. The volcanic rocks below were dark and alien-looking, water crashing against them endlessly. The salt smell was strong here, carried on the wind that no longer felt quite so hostile thanks to the heaters.

They watched the light change, the sky turning from pale blue to pink at the edges. The ocean reflected the colours, turning gold where the sun touched it. Jay had seen sunsets before, but this place made it feel different, more vivid.

They stayed on the terrace until the light began to fade. The temperature dropped with the sun, but the heaters and blankets kept them comfortable. Jay felt utterly content, the disasters of the morning hike forgotten in the warmth and beauty of the afternoon.

The adventurous group returned shortly before dinner, shaking from the cold, red-faced and grinning. Even Ray looked accomplished, which was remarkable.

"That was incredible. The most beautiful ridge view I've ever seen.," Zoe announced.

"It was freezing," Luc added.

"We need sauna. Immediately. Before dinner. Nothing heats better than sauna. We need to unfreeze our cores," Ray said.

Everyone stopped and stared. Ray barely spoke, but here he was, actively demanding sauna time, looking almost animated about it.

"Sauna it is," Max agreed, clearly not wanting to miss this unprecedented moment.

The first time Ray had shown enthusiasm for anything social, no one was going to refuse. The group reconvened, everyone heading to their rooms to prepare. Jay walked beside Ian, feeling his stomach start to tie itself in knots.

"The sauna is communal?" he asked.

"Yes."

"And. Traditional. In the northern style."

"Naked, yes."

Sauna culture wasn't big in Jay's hometown. He knew it was popular in the north, he'd just never actually done it. Sitting naked next to coworkers seemed like a big ask. Sitting naked next to Ian seemed impossible.

In their room, he changed into a robe, trying to think of an excuse to skip it. A year ago, this would have paralysed him. The anxiety would have won, hands down. But now? His insecurities were still there, still whispering, but this was socially acceptable time with Ian naked. When else would this opportunity present itself? The excitement of the moment overtook every other consideration. The shame, the fear—they were irrelevant in the face of that.

He followed Ian down to the sauna complex. The facility was beautiful, with multiple rooms at different temperatures. They gathered in the changing area, everyone stripping off robes and wrapping towels around their waists for the walk to the hot room.

The sauna itself was large enough to accommodate their group comfortably. Ray had chosen the hottest one, the temperature gauge reading ninety degrees. Jay could feel the heat radiating out even before they entered.

Inside, wooden benches lined the walls in tiers. Everyone claimed spots, and then the towels came off. Jay kept his eyes straight as he removed his own towel and sat down on the second tier, trying to look like this was completely normal and not at all terrifying.

Thankfully the heat of the place hid his crimson blush well.

He stared determinedly at the ceiling. The wood was beautiful, expertly fitted. Very interesting wood. Fascinating wood, really.

From the corner of his eye, he could see Ian sitting nearby. Completely naked. Casually reclining against the wall like this was the most natural thing in the world. Ian looked relaxed, elegant, completely unbothered. Beautiful.

Jay's eyes darted over before he could stop himself. Ian's body was perfectly sculpted, lean muscle definition from years of training. The gym had done its work. He looked like he belonged in marble.

Jay snapped his eyes back to the ceiling. This was mortifying. 

This was also thrilling, making him feel guilty and excited and terrified all at once. Ian's body, completely exposed to his eyes, made his heart hammer so loudly he was certain everyone could hear it. The heat made it worse, his pulse racing, his skin flushed. This view was going to haunt his dreams for the rest of his life.

"This is exactly what we needed," Ray broke the silence.

"Agreed. I can feel my core temperature coming back," Zoe replied.

Jay focused on their voices, trying to distract himself. This was normal. People did this all the time. He wasn't dying, even though it felt like he might be.

"How hot does this get?" Rin asked.

"Up to a hundred degrees. But ninety is optimal for recovery."

The conversation continued, but Jay contributed nothing, too busy trying not to stare at Ian while also trying not to make it obvious that he was trying not to stare.

"You alright?" Ian asked at one point.

"Fine. Just hot."

"That's the point."

"Right. Yes. The point."

Jay returned his attention to the ceiling, where it would stay for the foreseeable future, and tried to remember how to breathe normally.

* * *

#### Pitiful plea

After the sauna, dinner was a natural conclusion to an exhausting day. The food was hearty and filling—roasted pheasants with root vegetables, thick gravy, more of that wonderful herbal bread.

Rin was recounting their trail adventures. "And then Ray—Ray—just stripped down and plunged straight into this wild pond. No hesitation. Just dove right in."

Luc nodded along. "I thought he was joking. He was not joking."

"He went under completely. I've never seen him look that happy," Zoe added.

Ray looked completely unperturbed by the storytelling. "Wild pools are even better than the facility ones. More minerals, less processing."

"It smelled like sulphur," Rin complained.

"That's the minerals."

"It was awful. Like rotten eggs."

Ray just shrugged and returned to his food.

Rin laughed again, clearly delighted by the entire experience. The team smiled, enjoying the unusual glimpse into Ray's passions.

Jay picked at his pheasant, only half-listening. The sauna had left him feeling strange. He kept seeing it. Ian's body, completely bare, enchanting and irresistible. The image refused to leave. Every time he closed his eyes it was there, burned into his mind. He needed to stop thinking about it.

He reached for his wine glass and drank more than he meant to. When no one was looking he found a bottle of liquor on the side table and poured a generous measure into his glass. The alcohol hit him faster than expected, the sauna apparently having destroyed what little tolerance he had. His limbs started to feel heavy.

By the middle of dinner, everything had gone blurry. Jay stared at his half-eaten pheasant and tried to remember why he'd stopped eating it. He wanted to eat more, but the fork was difficult to manage.

Ian turned to look at him. "Jay?"

"Mmm?"

"Are you alright?"

"Fiiiine."

"How much did you drink?"

"Little bit. Maybe. More than little bit."

"I think we should step outside for some air."

"'M fine."

"You're not fine. Come on."

Jay didn't answer. He was focused on his plate, trying to spear a piece of potato. Ian got up and addressed the table. "We're heading back. He needs to sleep this off."

"This is becoming a tradition," Luc observed.

"Seems like it."

Max raised his glass in a mock salute. "Good luck."

Ian helped him stand. Jay's legs worked, but only just. Everything tilted sideways. He held onto Ian's arm and concentrated on walking.

They made it to the lobby before Ian paused. "Let's get some fresh air. It might help."

He guided Jay toward the entrance, and they stepped outside into the freezing night. The cold was shocking after the warmth of the dining room. Above them, stars scattered across the sky in impossible numbers, burning fiercely against the darkness.

"Oh," Jay breathed, staring up. Then the cold registered fully and he whimpered, pressing against Ian. "Cold. It's so cold."

He clung to Ian desperately, trying to burrow into his warmth. The embrace was clumsy, but he didn't care. Ian was warm and Jay was freezing. Ian's arms came around him. For a moment they stood like that, Jay plastered against Ian's chest, shivering. Ian seemed torn, his hold uncertain, like he couldn't decide whether to push Jay away or pull him closer.

"We need to get you inside," Ian said finally. 

He steered Jay back through the lobby and down the hallway to their suite. Jay stumbled along beside him, still holding onto Ian's sleeve with both hands.

In the room, Ian tried to guide him toward the bed. "Come on. You need to sleep this off."

But Jay pulled back, refusing. He sat down on the floor instead and stayed there, looking up at Ian with unfocused eyes.

"Jay, get up."

"No."

"You can't sleep on the floor."

"Don't wanna."

Ian crouched beside him, looking concerned. "What's wrong? Tell me what I can do to help."

"Nothing. There's nothing you can do."

"That's not true. I'll do anything. Just tell me what you want."

"I want you to hold me for the rest of my life," Jay whispered.

Ian froze.

Jay looked at him through the fog of alcohol, seeing Ian's expression, and felt his heart crack. "It's okay. I know it's too much. I know. Just—just for tonight? Please? Just tonight and I won't ask again, I promise."

His vision was blurring. He might have been crying, he wasn't sure, and Ian was looking at him like Jay had said something incomprehensible.

"Please," Jay whispered. "Just tonight."

"Alright. Alright, I've got you."

Ian cursed himself as he gathered Jay into his arms, moving him to the bed. He lay down with Jay still clinging to him, arranging them both on top of the covers, Jay's head tucked against his shoulder.

"There. I'm holding you. Is this what you wanted?"

"Yeah. This is—this is amazing. Don't need anything else. Ever."

Jay made himself comfortable against Ian's body, fitting himself into the spaces between them. Ian's arms were tight around him. This was more than he'd ever dared hope for.

"This is amazing. Feels so good. Don't need anything else. Just this. Just you."

Ian's hold tightened. "Shh. Just rest."

Jay felt Ian's hand come up to stroke through his hair. The motion was soothing, hypnotic. Jay's eyes drifted closed. The alcohol and exhaustion and warmth all combined, pulling him down into sleep.

Ian held him for a long time after Jay's breathing evened out into sleep. He lay there in the dark room, arms around Jay, feeling the rise and fall of his chest. Jay had curled into him trustingly, completely relaxed now.

Hold me for the rest of my life.

The words echoed in Ian's head, playing on repeat. He knew it was just drunk rambling, likely triggered by the atmosphere, the exhaustion, the wine on top of the sauna. It didn't mean anything. Jay wouldn't even remember saying it tomorrow.

It was still the most beautiful thing Ian had ever heard.

He wanted to kiss Jay's hair. The temptation was almost painful. Just a small gesture, barely anything. But he couldn't. He had already crossed too many lines tonight, if he took more it would become something he couldn't take back.

He stayed like that for what felt like hours, savouring the weight of Jay against him, the warmth of his body, the sounds of his breathing. This moment, this closeness—it was something Ian would never forget, even knowing he shouldn't have it.

Finally, when he was certain Jay was deeply asleep, Ian extracted himself. He laid Jay down properly, wrapped him in a blanket, made sure he was comfortable and warm, then he forced himself to move away. He went to his own bed and lay down, staring at the ceiling in the darkness.

Sleep was impossible. His mind kept replaying the evening—Jay clinging to him in the cold, the desperate plea for Ian to hold him, the weight of him in Ian's arms. Rest of my life, Jay had said.

Ian closed his eyes and let himself imagine it, just for a moment. A life where Jay meant those words. Where Ian could hold him not just for one night but for all the nights to come. Where this closeness was allowed, accepted. The fantasy hurt more than it comforted.

Ian opened his eyes again, banishing the thoughts. Tomorrow Jay would wake up embarrassed, probably not remembering clearly what he'd said. They would pretend it hadn't happened. Everything would go back to normal.

In the darkness of this room with Jay sleeping peacefully nearby, Ian let himself feel the full weight of what he wanted and couldn't have, the love he'd been suppressing for so long, the hope he kept trying to suffocate.

Hold me for the rest of my life.

"I would," Ian whispered into the darkness. "God help me, I would."

* * *

#### Cruel joke

Jay woke with a faint headache and the vague sense that he had done something terrible.

He kept his eyes closed, trying to piece together the previous night through fragments of memory. Cold air. Stars burning overhead. Warmth. Ian's scent. The feel of being held.

Oh no.

Jay curled into a ball, pulling the blanket over his head. Had he clung to Ian? Had he actually done that? The memory was hazy but undeniable. He'd grabbed onto Ian outside, desperate and pathetic, seeking warmth like a child.

He groaned and pulled at his hair through the blanket. He was an imbecile. A complete and utter imbecile.

What else had he done? What had he said? The memories were frustratingly incomplete, sliding away when he tried to focus on them. Just impressions. The security of Ian's arms around him.

He needed to face this. Whatever damage he'd done, he had to make it right. He sat up slowly and opened his eyes properly. The room was bright. His bed was rumpled from restless sleep. And Ian—

Ian was already up, sitting on the balcony in the freezing weather. A coffee cup sat on the table beside him. He looked like a statue, completely still, staring out at the ocean. It felt like a very bad omen.

On the nightstand beside Jay's bed sat a glass of water and two painkillers.

He took the painkillers, drank the water, and headed to the bathroom. The shower was scalding hot, an attempt to wash away the shame and confusion. He stood under the spray, trying to organize his thoughts.

He'd overstepped badly. He'd made Ian uncomfortable, imposed on him, created a situation that Ian had been too kind to refuse. The worst part was not knowing exactly what he'd done. The gaps in his memory were full of potential disasters.

When he finally emerged from the bathroom, he found Ian exactly where he'd left him. Still on the balcony. Still frozen in place.

Jay's resolve hardened. He wanted nothing more than to hide and never show his face again, but more than anything, he needed to apologize. Ian didn't deserve to face such indignation from his own assistant. Jay would accept whatever punishment came. He'd leave if Ian wanted. He'd report himself to HR. Whatever it took to atone.

He stepped onto the freezing balcony, the cold immediately biting through his sweater. His wet hair made it worse, but he didn't let it deter him.

"Ian, I'm so sorry. I don't remember everything from last night, but I know I—"

Ian cut him off immediately. "You didn't do anything wrong."

"But I—"

"You were drunk. Floppy and mumbling, but otherwise very manageable. You cried a lot, though. Apparently you're a sad drunk."

"I cried?" Jay didn't remember crying. That was somehow more mortifying than if he'd been rowdy or ridiculous. 

"Yes. I had to lull you to sleep."

"I'm sorry."

"Don't be."

"Then why are you out here?"

"I have a strange headache. Feels more like a migraine. I thought the cold would help, but it hardly did."

"Oh. I'm still sorry. For crying. For being a burden."

"You weren't a burden. Come inside. You're going to catch pneumonia."

Back in the warmth of the room, Ian closed the balcony door and turned to face Jay directly. "Nothing happened, Jay. You were upset, I helped you to bed. That's all. You don't need to apologize."

But Jay wasn't entirely convinced. Maybe Ian was just being kind, protecting Jay from the full embarrassment of whatever he'd done.

They packed in near silence, checking the room for forgotten items, zipping up suitcases. At breakfast, fragments of the team were scattered around the tables. Zoe and Luc had apparently left early for another short hike at the cliffs. Ray was in the hot springs with Rin, taking advantage of every last moment. The rest were finishing their food.

"There he is. How's the head?" Max called out when Jay appeared.

"Fine," Jay lied.

"You were pretty far gone last night. We were taking bets on whether you'd make it to breakfast."

"The sauna must have lowered my alcohol tolerance. I barely remember the evening."

"Classic."

After breakfast, Ian suggested they get a hot stone massage. They spent an hour in the spa. Jay felt better afterwards, less panicked about the previous night.

They soaked in one of the pools afterwards, but the peace Jay had felt yesterday was missing. He kept watching Ian, searching for clues about what had really happened.
